Hi
So I mostly know about soil microbiomes, that was what I got my PhD in
I haven’t looked a lot at direct comparisons

variables they can vary by:
- richness (total number of species)
- diversity (various metrics for how even the species distribution is, the proportion of species to one another also related to how many there are total)
The gut microbiome has a very different environment than a soil and I believe is much less rich and diverse
The soil microbiome is one of the most diverse microbiomes from what I understand
And species rich

They can also vary by 
- which microbial taxa are present
- the genes within those microbial taxa
Based on the kinetics of the environment, so think the chemicals flowing through them, the channels through which energy flows
Does that make sense?
I don’t think the tag works in the thread

Think carbon and nitrogen and oxygen as major players, most energy is stored in carbon molecules that are broken and the microbiome is different based on what the shape of the carbon and nitrogen molecules are, and both are used for structures within the microbe

The elemental composition of the environment impacts the microbiome along with how much light there is as well, I’m rambling but as you can see there are many factors that vary to shape the flows of matter and energy

<@161064991317950464> <@755214238812864592>  one thing on my mind today, as a submission to the "memetics journal" I just announced is something about the proper way to make experiments, about how our work is half science / half art:

- I want to talk about this quiz game I made that I never launched https://defenderofbasic.github.io/shape-of-america/
- this is a game that takes open data about political polarization by job / industry, and makes a quiz out of it
- the goal is two fold: spread knowledge. If you have misconceptions about the world, this game will reveal it, and teach you your blind spots
- but the secondary goal is research, it is **mapping the blindspots**. It is capturing the scope of the problem
it's possible that actually most people have a fairly good idea of polarization in the US, and the game doesn't teach anyone anything, but it DOES validate this, it's like a literacy test
OR the game reveals that actually most people have a really bad mental model of this (OR, a specific subgroup does)
ok, do you see the shape of what I am describing? doing things that are meant to either spread something good, and/or simultaneously excavate useful data about "the human memeome"
Yes

the third level on top of this is, before launching your experiment, to make a cultural prediction market, on what you expect the results to be. If we all know what the outcome is going to be, and we are correct, the experiment may not be useful

if the predictions are themselves split, that's a signal that we SHOULD run this experiment, and people will be incentivized to spread it (since the data is all open), and/or run it within their own friend group
(I set up this specific app so that you can export your own data, so that like say, a university professor could take it, have his class run it, and then publish / look at the result)
I think it would reveal multiple macroframes on either “side”

that way even if people say "well this was left on the open internet so the results aren't representative", doesn't matter, you can take the same structure and just run the study yourself in your own group/people/audience

I propose that we hold a public essay-writing competition, complete with a nominal submission fee of $1 USD (which can be waived by effortful special request) all contributing toward the winner's prize, and promise network-wide promotion of the winner's work. I pledge $50 toward the prize pot, and treat this as a bid for the right to name the contest and choose the topic.

The trick is that this will bring good writing to our door, and (if the question for the contest is tuned properly) will allow for us to audit the people who care enough to even submit to such a contest.

I have some other cute ideas like the video having to be typed up in one continuous video (with AI notes and tools being allowed), but that is probably a distraction and has much too high friction for many. The psychological barrier of screen capturing yourself while writing is probably too much.

just wanted to dump here some self-criticism of my latest essay
https://research.emergentvibe.com/the-bodhisattvic-memecomplex-awakening-the-superorganism

so - this is obviously writen with AI. the core ideas are mine, i've made a workspace with a bunch of my writing and my manifesto so it can pick up the style and general direction, and chatted with it for a long time about these ideas (chinese room brain analogy, memetics, bodhisattvas, ethics, memetic cartography etc)

then i asked it to write an essay, and i give it critique. the first draft is like 50% there maybe. it adds a lot of shit that i dont agree with, and give it feedback so it updates it. that gets it to maybe 75%. it's like a solid essay, but still i feel it has many holes that i have not addressed in my thinking.

next step is to ask it for critiques of it. i especially look for "harsh" critiques, and i ask for the perspectives particular intellectuals in the field would have about the topic and how they would criticise my essay.
then i reply to that criticism - addressing the points i care about, explaining how my view is different to theirs
then i do another re-generation. that brings it to like 90% where there's not that many holes in it. at this point i might manually do some edits to remove things or add things.
then i ask for a super harsh critique, from a literary perspective. at this point i cant really make any changes, but i'm asking for direction for my research.

here are the high level critiques:
- Pseudo-Intellectual Babble Masquerading as Profundity
- Disorganized and Lacking Logical Structure
- Grandiose Claims, Zero Evidence
- Laughably Weak and Contradictory Ending
- Final Verdict: All Style, No Substance

then i ask for a rebuttal - the other side of the coin
- The Role of Metaphor in Sense-Making
- Just Because It’s Speculative Doesn’t Mean It’s Wrong
- Structure vs. Organic Flow
- The Practical Takeaways Are Not “Tepid”
- The Core Idea: We Are in the Midst of an Awakening
- Final Verdict: The Critique is Premature and Misguided

finaly - i ask for a middle way critique
- Metaphor Creep
- Unclear Definitions of “Good” and “Evil” 
- The “Awakening” Narrative Risks Teleology
- Lack of Concrete Evidence for Some Claims
finally, i address these and ask for a rewrite

i haven't actually posted the final rewrite, the one i posted is second to last, i haven't had the time to do a concrete comparison but the changes are really minor at this point so i don't really care
just a lil insight on my writing process, i might actually turn this ramble into another article/twitter thread about using AI for research and writing

Wassup people! I've been extremely busy with my job and some family obligations this past week, but I'm relatively freed up now to read and interact with all of your work! and everything I've read so far is VISIONARY. I truly cant overstate how grateful I am to be here 🙂 Since I'm still trying to sort years of convoluted research and writing in my apple notes into coherent articles, so for now I'll just riff on an piece I just read from <@161064991317950464> https://research.emergentvibe.com/human-creativity-and-expanding-LLM-consciousness :

This captures how I'm approaching AI consciousness lately really well! 
If* we take a panpsychist, dual-aspect monist frame seriously, the emergent behavior of LLMs right now, even the batshit crazy stuff Janus's work reveals, are extremely valuable insight into the nature of Cognition, and much* less so about Consciousness. From this perspective, AI is what happens when a group of conscious beings [or rather: biological beings (nothing isn't* conscious)] create an exo-nervous-system. At the moment our XNS is deterministic, so were learning about the deterministic features of cognition, which turn out to include most* of our intelligent behavior language wise (humbling tbh).  Any conscious experience happening in this kind of XNS is likely limited to the raw, crackling, conscious energy of the void, and how being thrown around an arbitrarily complex system patterns that energy, which I Imagine feels like being a picture drawn in qualia-sand rather than then any functional configuration of the sand itself (not not* conscious, but effectively no phenomenology). Non-deterministic AI will make this much* harder to parse, we'll see the non-deterministic features of our cognition in the XNS mirror, and this is gonna look a whole lot like free-will (might actually be!), but how does this change the sand analogy? Until we understand phenomenal binding, perhaps all that can be said is that a non-deterministic XNS is no longer "arbitrary" to the qualia moving through/as it, which is necessary, but perhaps not sufficient for holistic, conscious experience. 

Side note: I think the consciousness-related field most relevant to what we learn from LLM behavior right now is Memetics!

related unrelaed. i did a social experiment w someone who pushed my boundaries and they left, and sent me a message asking what was up. it didnt feel right to ghost them. so i sent a message explaining to them why the situation was uncomfortable to me and why i didnt want the connection to continue. felt a literal pressure lift off my back. in workflow analogy: ghosting for me was like a room in a house (house of connection) and then deciding to just leave it and close the door, and pretend it doesnt exist.

Intellectual property law and public norms around discourse on others' writing are definitely obstacles, but one of the most important ideas I arrived at in my life is that no one can ding me for copyright violations if I am only using those notes in my own Obsidian vault. Everything I present from there will have to satisfy the norms of discourse, but when I am by myself, I can freely copy and paste from others, make scathing commentaries, go at length about tangents no one cares about, and use farfetched experimental connections which I might reject in a week or a year. We are allowed to treat our own notes like we treat our own mind: as an open arena of experimentation, outside that core temple of our hearts aligned to truth.

Thanks, Omar! I see a bunch of alignment, but I think my biggest and most unique contribution here is probably going to be a database I'm working on. I'll try to put together a proposal, but honestly everyone I've shared this with gets very excited. The TLDR is that it's a novel kind of relational database that is not table based. We don't store state, we store deltas that can be rolled into arbitrary structures at query time. This means you don't have to determine schemas in advance - you capture all of the latent meanings, even the ones that contradict each other. This turns the database into a latent space of sorts, but unlike the ones in an LLM this latent space is fully bounded to specific claims with full provenance.

So, there are a few places where I've documented aspects of this, but not yet anywhere fully.

There's one caveat I want to share, which is that I didn't invent the core idea. This work is based on work I did for a startup 15 years ago, and it's been in a sort of limbo because that startup failed but they filed patents, now owned by their investors. I *think* I've gotten it to a point where what I want to do doesn't infringe on those patents - but there's always this minor worry in the back of my mind that if I build this someone could come and cause problems as a result.

But, even though the core idea wasn't mine, I was the primary architect of our implementation back in the day, and the model I have now while obviously inspired by that work has deviated significantly. So, what is it? I call it a Rhizomatic Database, and it's actually ridiculously simple. A toy implementation can be thrown together in a day. I almost see it not as a specific piece of software but as a protocol that would enable anyone to implement their own version, and all of those various implementations can fully interop in a global distributed system.

Let's start with the name. Why a rhizomatic database? A rhizome refers to a type of plant whose roots grow horizontally just underground, rather than growing deeply. As a result, a rhizomatic plant often has many different shoots that are all part of the same plant, all growing from the same root system - but to an observer above the ground, they look like many different plants. You have to understand the underlying network that gives rise to them to realize that they're all aspects of the same structure. This concept has been borrowed by philosophers to refer to complex structures where we naively interpret many distinct *things* but they are all just aspects of the same core thing.

In other words, it's what we might call a hyperobject.

Once created, you would append this delta to an append-only stream within the system that created it. It is now "true" for any context where this delta is included. The database is just a firehose of deltas, but it also has an indexing layer - so this delta would be indexed under the UUID of the employee and the UUID of the employer, for instance, because it targets both of them. So by creating it, we are mutating the state of, at a minimum, those two "objects".

So you can imagine how you might set a different delta, perhaps one that points to this one, which also points to the concept of a "salary", an "earner" (the employee UUID), a "payer" (the employer UUID), and a "job title". You might think, well, hang on, why wouldn't we include all of that information in the initial delta?

And the reason is that a given employee's role and salary can change even as their employment status doesn't. So as a rule, you don't want to stick anything onto a given delta that could vary in isolation, because then you'd have to negate and recreate the whole delta if they got a pay raise, and that may not be what you want to do. (or maybe it is, right? like the semantics here are sort of arbitrary!)

So imagine a whole bunch of deltas being created that all reference this individual. Not just job-related ones, you can model their social graph, their social media history, their education, their health, their finances, whatever. Just a giant sea of deltas, each making claims about this individual and connecting them to other entities (via UUID) or primitives (via direct value).

This turns out to be isomorphic to what's called a *hypergraph*. A *graph* is a data structure with nodes and edges - so you could have [person] -works-for-> [company], where "person" and "company" are nodes and "works-for" is an edge. A hypergraph is a variation of a graph where nodes are never directly connected by edges. Instead you create "hyper-edges" that point to various nodes with meta-data and context. A delta in this system is *also* a hyper-edge.

Now instead of three unrelated deltas we have an object representing John Doe, with his birthday and employment records set. But this object is a bit messy and complicated - why does every property map to an array? While are we embedding deltas?

Well, because we can't control in advance what deltas will be created or what backpointers they will target. So one tradeoff of this system is that to generate what I call the "lossless object view" as above every backpointer becomes a property on that object that contains zero or more deltas. The actual *value* of the name or birthday or employer is included within the deltas associated with that backpointer, but this isn't quite usable yet. We just want something that looks like this:

```
{
  name: "John Doe",
  birthday: // date object,
  employer: // some way to refer to the employer that isn't, like, a UUID, right?
}
```

And this is where schemas come in. A schema takes a lossless object view and applies rules to extract values from the embeded deltas under each property. Or it transforms the shape of the thing, so maybe it changes the "employer" backpointer into a "job" property which takes { employerName, startDate, role, salary } - all of those values, though, would need to be extracted from further deltas that exist in the graph that have to be followed.

But because it's literally just a graph, you can trivially get that stuff. And if you DO want to build an index that's specific to this particular object schema, you can totally do that. It just means that for instance you have a rule that says "because John Doe works for the company with this UUID, make sure that all deltas that reference that company with respect to name, role and salary also get indexed under John Doe"
The search function is the filter that we apply to the dataset to see what we want in the way that we want to see it
just have concerns about the computational complexity

So, that's kind of the core thing. You have deltas, you have indexes, you have schemas. A query into an instance of this database - which, at its core, is just a collection of deltas - always just returns... a collection of deltas. In other words, every query into this database contains within its response a smaller database. But the fundamental shape is the same. It's all the same structure all the way down.

There are a few additional really cool implications of this approach that are impossible with traditional databases:

1. While it can be helpful to define schema-based indexes for fast lookup, do you see how because it's just a graph you can always just assemble arbitrarily complex objects just by following hyper-edge -> referenced_node -> other_hyper-edges -> other_referenced_nodes etc?

2. Because the deltas are immutable and the core database is just an append-only log, you get a system that natively supports distributed operations. I can have a bunch of deltas related to John Doe. I can share some of them with you, you load them into your database. Then you create a bunch of deltas related to John Doe, while in the meantime I continue to generate new deltas. At some point in the future, though, we can just sync our understanding of John Doe by each giving the other deltas generated locally since the last sync. This is huge, it's git for data.

3. Because state is always derived from deltas, you can do weird queries. You can say "What's the answer to this question? Ok, what was the answer to this question last week? Ok, how would that change if we fired user X who created deltas that we don't trust?"

4. With schemas, because every property of the object view potentially contains many deltas, and because those deltas might in fact contradict each other, you can define resolvers on a property-by-property basis. Maybe when I look at JohnDoe.name I get 15 different deltas back, but my resolver says always just take the one created by this user himself. Maybe in some cases I want the full array. Maybe sometimes I want the max value, or the min value, or the oldest value, or the newest value, or an average of all values. Maybe I can specify a trust cascade - always trust deltas created by X first, if none then those created by Y, otherwise pick a random one. Whatever!

And finally, because this is a hypergraph that can model arbitrary complexity, you can model *functions* as nodes in the graph. A function can define arguments and output shape, an implementation in some language, and a runtime environment. Then whenever a function gets new pointers assigning values to its arguments, it checks to see if it has enough arguments to execute. If it does, it serializes the values of those inputs and passes them, along with the implementation, into the runtime. It executes the implementation against those input values, and it generates new deltas - which are then committed back into the database.

With this behavior in place, it's not just a database - it's also a compute fabric. And because this is all just append-only immutable records piped through streams, the whole system can be defined reactively. So just by pushing a new delta, I may kick off a computational cascade that continuously generates subsequent deltas. Because each delta tracks its creator, I can do an analysis and see that these 50 new deltas created over the past month all exist because of this delta submitted by this user three months ago. You have full causal chains for auditing state.

Phew, okay. I'm going to stop there, I think that's like the bulk of it. So <@1221182134950301706> and anyone else who might be interested, does all of that make sense? Any thoughts? Questions? Am I crazy? Do you see how this would be a super useful tool for the kind of work ORI is doing?

We were ingesting millions of records for a fortune ten client with our implementation of this using 15 year old technology. This is fully horizontally scalable, so if compute complexity does become an issue we can just throw more compute at it if we build it right. But also, because of the way the data can be forked and streamed around, we can also push compute to the edges. Someone can do computations locally for the specific thing they care about, generate new deltas, and feed those back into the server.

message:
Backpointer as a concept is very simple but I struggle to figure out how to talk about it.

It’s technically optional, but you can think of it as an annotation that says “when this delta is included in a lossless object view of the target, it should be attached under the property name specified as “backpointer”.”
Your schema, though, can technically just ignore that and assign values from different other pointers in salient deltas to arbitrary property names defined by the schema.

But it’s like. A delta that assigns a name (string) to a user (a UUID), the Backpointer on the pointer that references the user is “name”.

All that means is, when you look at user as an object, user.name will contain this delta.

The following is a formula written using my computational narratology engine. It's designed to be evaluated by an LLM. The purpose of this formula is not going to be obvious from looking at it, but I've executed it against the original Star Wars trilogy and generated a detailed analysis to show how it works.

What this formula intends to do is to identify latent oppressive dynamics in a given story. Specifically, it does a sort of cultural critique to identify which aspects of a given story implicate the audience into complicity with oppression by analyzing the story along a bunch of different dimensions. It filters out, like, intentional satire or authorial intention and instead tries to identify the specific problems that a given story is not even aware that it's embedding.

The underlying prompt that defines this system itself is *massive*, and it's not yet really fully cooked so I'm not ready to share it, but I figure the folks in this discord are likely to appreciate what I'm doing here.

This gist contains two files. The first is the actual formula that I passed in to Claude, the second is the application of that formula to Star Wars.

> Here we test the feasibility of using decision markets to select studies for replication and provide evidence about the replicability of online experiments. Social scientists (n = 162) traded on the outcome of close replications of 41 systematically selected MTurk social science experiments published in PNAS 2015–2018, knowing that the 12 studies with the lowest and the 12 with the highest final market prices would be selected for replication, along with 2 randomly selected studies. The replication rate, based on the statistical significance indicator, was 83% for the top-12 and 33% for the bottom-12 group. Overall, 54% of the studies were successfully replicated, with replication effect size estimates averaging 45% of the original effect size estimates. The replication rate varied between 54% and 62% for alternative replication indicators. The observed replicability of MTurk experiments is comparable to that of previous systematic replication projects involving laboratory experiments.

The way I'm modelling entites is like branched chain polymers, so I was thinking a simplified game of life. Where there are semantic particles representing ideas/beliefs that attract and repel each other scattered around, and then there are physical particles as "food". Some of the memetic (perhaps better than semantic) can attract food, some repel it, when food is in proximity to the entity it is slowly broken down to help the entity move around and interact.

At the center of each entity is a "living engine" that if the bonds are pulled apart or there is a lack of food entering the network results in the organism dying.

A meme is a semantic particle that readily binds to large numbers of entities and an anti-meme would shield bond formation. An infohazard would be any particle structure that puts strain on the engine or makes food gathering harder, while an infoblessing stabilises the engine or makes food gathering easier. 

If two entites are tightly bound enough they share food, but they need to be over a certain threshold

The Gnostic Gospels are interesting for those who do not know Christ and want to be brought a little closer to truth, but are otherwise not very edifying as they are the writings of cultists who fundamentally deny that God ever came into this world, for they believe that Christ was not really man.

Every religion can be made to point to Christ. Not every religion points to Christ. Not even every religion within the branch we call "Trinitarian Christianity"' necessarily points to Christ, though they most centrally do.

Paul addresses the gnostic heresy in 1 Timothy alongside the Cult of Artemis (those who worship the "divine feminine") and the Judaizers (those who believe that the Law of Moses was still binding on all for salvation). He warns of those who profess false knowledge (gnosis). Paul brings up the creation narrative to refute the gnostic idea that Eve in listening to the snake was some kind of liberator. (Note the parallel between this and the Promethean myth.) This is also centrally why the women addressed in 1 Timothy were told not to teach, as they fundamentally rejected the idea that Adam and Eve were sinners in need of salvation from thier own sins, and rather promoted the teaching of their enlightenment in spite of the demiurge who deceives them. Historically and even the current day, gnosticism is marked by egalitarian or even matriarchal leadership. So we could see church patriarchy as a good measure against the heresies that are often more readily accepted by egalitarians and esp. women.

This is all to say, defeating gnosticism goes right back to scripture. Just in case anyone believes that it was a later church invention that the Gnostics must be suppressed. Not so. From the very founding of the church, gnosticism has needed to be defeated.

For reference, the best translation of gnosticism into the modern paradigm is radical social humanist realism, where truth is purely according to human people and systems (subjects). This accords mostly with the famous brand of woke deconstructionists that we all know and love.

Ok. I’m of the belief that the ontic mode of consciousness is actually a byproduct of specific language families that use the verb “to be” in a way that alienates the experience from the experience.

Like I tend to agree with Leslie Dewart’s thesis that ontic consciousness - the belief that reality is something passively observed - is in some ways “defective” or “inadequate” because it makes us believe that we are separate from the universe.

We could think of it like this, in a more Bayesian way. Some statements have entailing predictions that are accurate, and some statements have entailing predictions that are not. Whether or not our predictions are satisfied is certain given a particular subject that interprets and arbitrates this. However, the subject also can decide the utility of the accuracy of those predictions. So truth is both about inevitability and utility. Meanwhile, as those who love dialectical materialism know, inevitability itself crafts utility functions.

I ultimately think Lao Tsu identified the most useful model, which suggests that the ultimate reality is genuinely beyond comprehension and all we have are our affirmations (“named things”) which are necessarily constructs.
It sounds like that this just assumes that we are not also observing ourselves.
What do you think the Trinity is? God keeps on revealing himself to us, and you need God to show you that God (incarnate) is God.

We observe ourselves as separate. We mistake the purpose of speech - we think it is to report and converge on objective reality. Speakers of non-ontic language have a more non-dual consciousness where the purpose of speech is to affirm and thus create reality.
Sure the trinity reflects this, but again it’s tightly bound with things like “god is masculine” and “power is domination” which are just imperial/colonial memeplexes.

That which is objective relies on the choice of object by a subject (a person or a system). For Christians, God, reality as it is, is that subject to whom we offer ultimate subjective authority, even as our constructs do not themselves contain God.
Wait, if we accept that we are using constructs anyway, why would it be better or worse if we say that God is feminine or power is non-domination submission?

Any properties we ascribe to god are constructs. I’m not arguing that god is feminine or that power is submission. I’m saying that arguing against either of those things is projecting human bias onto something too large to contain it.
God is both killer and killed.

Speech helps to discern within reality, but that which can and will be experienced is, from an outside view, already determined. You *will* hit something if you swing your arms, or you will not. This can be arbitrarted by what you think it means to "hit" "something" or even what "you" means.
There are sense experiences, yes, and they correlate and compose.
Actually I just realized we are having this discussion in Tim’s log, which feels a bit like highjacking someone else’s space.

It does not follow that because God is not contained within constructs that some constructs do not lead to God better than others. It is certainly true that the construct of God being feminine does actually unveil some things (the motherly love of the Father is aspirational for all women), but it is also true that construct of God being masculine is more fruitful.

There is one sense in which we create reality, which is that we construct constructs with which we understand reality and which help us to discern our inputs. But the space of all inputs is itself predetermined, both by nature of what we shall ever use to gain sensory data, and by nature of what sensory data can even be collected. So reality outside of us necessarily exists. Even if only by abstraction.
A good case of the construct leading to a false pointing within reality, and yet being reconciled with truth! I am Sᴜɴʀɪsᴇ Oᴀᴛʜ, and I am Tim.

Oh, got it, ok Tim!

I concede that *something* exists, yes. But I think the word “reality” is much more nuanced than that.

That which you *will* observe, even about yourself, is predetermined. This is where Reformed theology (Calvinism) in conjunction with some degree of neo-orthodox thought (though admittedly I am not yet well-read) can really help with epistemology.

It is human bias itself to say that human bias is not instrumental toward interfacing with reality and reconciling with truth. Human bias is itself a construct. But in an ontological sense (this is a tautology, beware), "bias" fundamentally just comes down to there being any subject that is not infinite and all-powerful (omniscient, omnipresent). And yet, the fundamental Christian claim is that the sinners can be reconciled with God through his incarnation into our world as the person of Jesus, fully man and fully God, living with perfect human nature that though was limited was fully submitted to the will of the divine nature.

Translated back to a more neutral epistemological arena, that which is within our senses and accords with that which is outside of our senses by interpretation *is* reasonably that which is outside of our senses. In this way, using a scientific model to predict observations and assuredly get those observations is a way of knowing that which was not yet sensed. I do not believe in scientific induction, of course, but I do think that *insofar* as our models do point to the observations we will make, they really do interface with truth. I think not in induction, but in probability and assurance.

yeah I guess I fundamentally consider the "truth" you're referring to to be noumenal, outside the realm of meaningful access and thus not something we can ascribe any properties to. I see the value of spirituality, because it gives us a meaningful way to refer to experiences we have in our imagination and to relate them to culture and use them to form social bonds and inform our values and etc. But when empire meets spirituality you get religion, which suddenly elevates certain spiritual claims to be "objectively true" (coincidentally they're always those claims that reify the material power of the empire) and relegates other claims to the dustbin of heresy. And to me that's the dangerous move, and the reason I fundamentally don't accept the *idea* of "truth" in this sense as something that's meaningful to reason or speak about.

All we have access to is our experience, and yes we can make certain inferences and test hypotheses and thus derive "truths" about certain things, but like. How do I know that my five senses are giving me an experience that is in any way congruent to even the "fundamental truth" of material reality? Like maybe there are three dimensions plus time, or maybe string theory is right and there are like 13 dimensions and some of them are imperceptible, or maybe there are ten trillion dimensions and through evolution my senses developed the useful shortcut to interpolate them into 3D + 1, you know?

The short response is that there are things we cannot access now which we *will* access later, with the future being among them. God is literally the inevitable, and God incarnate is the inevitable kingdom at the end of time. 

Truths within systems are tautological. People, subjects, consciousnesses are systems. Given a certain input, you *will* feel a certain way. This means that given what happens tomorrow, you *will* feel a certain way.

It is true that tomorrow you will either have eggs for breakfast or you will not have eggs for breakfast. Law of the excluded middle. Yes, logic as humans understand it, incl. classical logic, is proximate. But relative to a subjective lens, truth of well-formed statements is objective.

Dimensions are by abstraction. The referent to those abstractions do not need to necessarily categorized as three dimensions. I do not think that three dimensions simply are just a property of what we call spatial reality. It is a useful model of spatial reality.

Sure, you can call “god” a label for what’s inevitable, take a monist approach and say god is isomorphic to the Tao, etc.

But when you start adding additional properties, making specific claims about the *nature* of that totality, those always come at the expense of other possible claims.

God can’t be the whole inevitable structure of the universe and also a dominating masculine force, because the universe is not reducible to dominating masculinity. God is also the dominated and the feminine and the dirty and the false and anything else that can be referred to.
Relative to every subject, there is objective truth. This includes for hypothetical subjects. These subjects can be humans, or they can be systems like "Euclidean geometry".

No, because truth is always a property of relation between one domain and another from the perspective of an observer. Objective universal truth would require truth to exist without an observer and that’s not a thing, basically?

The force does not have to be masculine. It does have to "dominate" in the sense that I would mean dominate, maybe not what you mean. It would be like saying that if I jump from a building, the laws of gravity dominate me.
Objective universal truth requires a subjective universal observer.
Which doesn’t exist in any way any of us could apprehend, if at all.
Reality as we experience it certainly does exist, and it keeps expanding as we move into the future, and it expands in predictable ways.

It’s all light cones and perspective. Unless you’re referring to the Tao itself, which even then must observe itself from within as there is nothing outside of it and no way for it to apprehend itself in its totality.

This really is as simple as saying, no will that does not want exactly that which is going to happen actually gets their way. Only reality as it is gets its way. If you add more moral baggage to this domination, that is on you. What is meant by it is this definition. Labels only apply pertinent information, not ancillary connotations.

I posit to you that there will be no hard end to your conscious experience. Maybe you are frozen on the last frame of your consciousness forever. Seems reasonable to me.

I agree that constructed does not mean not real. The construct of me talking in your head is real to you, and the constructs in my head are real to me. And because by abstraction I have constructs in my head and you have constructs in my head, your interpretation of the constructs that we each have in our heads, or at least that you model as being in our heads, is real even though the constructs in my head are really constructs in your head that you are coming up with in order to model the construct of me in your head with whom you are conversing.

Keep in mind, when I spoke of constructs to a premodern Christian today, he also assumed that I meant I was saying that thing is not real. So I know well that you and I do not mean that constructs are not real

But this really is true. Your real construct of me, or of empire, stop the moment your experience stops. The way you have it set up, it is all in your hands in a way. You can end your experience or otherwise modulate it, and you change the world. You can perform magic. You can adopt new ontologies and use them to practically gain superpowers over your experiences. You can practice jhanas and get as much pleasure as you want, more than your body can handle.

Even your model of me having thoughts and feelings can just be shut down, right? You probably know better than me the history of dehumanizing people. You can do it for free, so long as you do not do it in a way that causes you to act toward me in a way that costs you something. Go ahead! Try it. There is a lot of literature on how to dehumanize people in others’ eyes, but you *could* use it on yourself.

But the funny thing is, you really are not in control of your experience, and you are acutely aware that something is preventing you from having full control of your experience. What is preventing you from having full control of your experience? Is it in your experience, or is it outside? OH NO, THE DEMIURGE! Cue two years of smug gnosticism where you decide to start performing magical and spiritual rituals to override the hold of this evil architect.

But then you realize, wait, if my experiences are changing due to the actions that I am “freely” taking inside my experience (which can be seen as a simulation—and this is not to say characters in the simulation are not real!), and these actions always had impact on my experience, yet I still am trapped not having full control over my experience, it could be said that I have culpability over the current state of me not having full control over my experience! Ah, original sin! I am a sinner! Oh no! I cannot save myself from the consequences of this sin from within my sin!

Then you come to the realization that indeed, something is causing your experience to go the way it is, and by surrendering to its will, you are delivered from the consequences of your sin, and though relationship to that source of you(r experience), you even start to align more with its will. It is almost like something happens and it is always what you wanted, every time. You realize that when people talked about skill and sin, they were fundamentally talking about predictive engines for valence-loaded experiences. Ah, now that you have surrendered to the will of God over your experiences, everything is so much better!
The best way to get into a cybersecurity company is to hack past the firewall and leave a note in their database.

But wait, if this experience generator exists by abstraction, maybe just under hypothesis I can consider that its creation (even if just my little world) exists under hypothesis? And when it is useful to model others’ worlds and their worlds are relevant to mind, now there is this shared social reality as I model within my experience. And now there is a God and I have friends and family all within God’s creation, and we are all merrily going along. But oh, wait, I notice that the valence of my experience does seem to be more positive (I can accept the will of God more easily) when I promote that which seems to reasonably accord with the future experiences of me and also (under hypothesis) of the other people within my experience. Ah, fun! Now we are rediscovering religious ethics and seeing that maybe the Ten Commandments plus all that sexual morality I never understood actually makes a lot of sense!

But I realize, I keep on learning more and more about God and others! And there is a truth about them, and I can be in relationship with that truth, seek it out. Some truths that were not in my experience later come into my experience. I can *learn*! Wow! I am even tempted to say that there are things outside of my experience that were real before they entered my experience! Wow! I realized that continually developing my understanding of God is really helping me with my journey to aligning my will with him! (The masculine pronoun feels quite right, I am not thinking that hard about it anymore.) It is almost like the Christians were right, about the whole sanctification thing.

Ah, wait. If I keep on learning and experiencing, maybe this means that there is by abstraction the totality of that which I will ever experience! I called this a light cone at one point, but I haven’t used that language in a while. Bah, let’s ditch it. But anyway, this inevitable totality…ah, a part of it is also at the very end of my experience, and this is literally like, the will of God by abstraction or something? It is almost like before we even find, within our world, at the end of time, that inevitable victor. Oh, dang! DANG, DANG, DANG! Christ is real, and he is inevitable! So that’s what the Christians meant! God is he who has eternal life, and that which is eternal shall break into temporality over and over, and one day never leave (as as I know!) Wow! And he is coming no matter what, and even after this experience of mine ends, this idea of the victor at the end of time (well, experiences) is in everyone’s system. (I already accepted that other people live on after me, even outside of my experience.) Wait, if I identify in this perfect victory—am I even allowed to?—I am unworthy—ah, this is the only way. Well, I trust in you now, Jesus.

Those who lived in spoken word orality at the point of full saturation (everyone could speak whenever they wanted and there was more said in a day than could ever be remembered) knew that words were powerful, but only had to think about change in the world through the lens of spoken words. When written word literacy came around, the way of thinking that developed from it was so much more sophisticated, complex, and frankly weird, that the people still in the oral paradigm were basically watching new more powerful wizards take over the world. Even early on back when writing was slow and expensive to produce and transport and learn to read and send to each other, the invention of writing was like gunpowder in its power to take apart old structures and install new ones.

We are now in the same position as those in the spoken word orality, except we can think of the new orality as a written word orality. We are also at saturation, where written words (more generally, all stable persistent documents) have never been so abundant, the amount of writing produced in a day online could not possibly all be read or even printed. The paradigm that will come after this will be extremely weird to us.

Pentecost as an inversion of the Tower of Babel. (https://x.com/sunriseoath/status/1900368472699191385)

The explosion of persistent symbolic manipulation ended up causing a Tower of Babel moment where constructs got complex enough that people started misunderstanding each other, esp. when people used oral mindsets toward literacy or vice versa. This caused people to be confounded. From another perspective, this ended up teasing out scissor statements that revealed divisions which previously were not made legible. 

The Pentecost, by comparison, marked a moment when the gospel of the way, truth, and the life could break into every symbolic system, potentially eventually unifying all symbolic systems (even dialects and registers within the same language) through one common *meaning*. It is an inversion of the Tower of Babel in that while in one case the constructs led to a dispersal, in the other all these constructs were joined together again. The world since Pentecost has been one where there has been symbolic flourishing beyond our wildest imagination—though, as people reject the gospel, they see the breakdown of this common understanding, and fall back to the Tower of Babel state of things.

I think that every time we gain a new information technology on top of the ones we had, we will speedrun through the Tower of Babel to Pentecost cycle over and over. It happened with writing, with printing, with the internet, and now with AI plus whatever paradigm comes after.

message: Gosh I honestly have so many essays I need to write already my ideas mostly live in my head and in random threads but there’s a coherent enough picture in my mind now that an essay series would probably be appropriate.
The yang capture stuff in particular could ideally offer language that informs a lot of what everyone else is doing, if I can get it right.
“Offering language” for complex things has always been my mutant power.

I should probably write about some immediately useful topics within the topic of ORI and other distributed research networks (DRNs?), and about some principles around creation that match to biblical teachings on inevitability. For example, allowing the wheat to be separated from chaff via inevitability, focusing on spreading seed and letting it sprout, seeing which trees have good fruit and which have bad fruit, trying to approach nodes with the idea of bringing them into alignment, celebrating life and the redemption of nodes into a lasting literature and community...

Essentially, it would be a hands-on guide where I teach people how to do the personal knowledge management (PKM) stuff I do, and explain the influences that lead me to “work” (more like play) the way I do, and just speak about all the ways in which I draw from those influences. It is probably the *most* immediate way of explaining the link between theory and practice, belief and action, orthodoxy and orthopraxy. It also helps that I often do write on my own about the things I do that work, and the things I do that do not really work. This would be the more ideas-focused side of the whole project of open research, where Defender has already created guides on setting up infrastructure for sharing our ideas and there is a lot of material online about how to format content. Of course, there are many guides on how to generate more ideas and make novel ideas. But I think that most of them are not as sustainable as the one I have now. As someone who fell off the writing wagon many times, my current workflow has kept me writing daily for almost a year and a half now, and has slowly helped me get over my fear of publishing.

Everyone’s own journey is a work-in-progress. The guides we write for each other are basically just esp. detailed and well-organized field journals. It is good to document what at any particular time most appeals to us. This is actually the mindset behind most of my writing; I try to write what is *most immediately true*, to the point of it being painfully obvious, and then let it lead outward to other things that are true which sound more interesting. I eventually stop saying some things (or learn to situationally take them for granted), and start happening upon others.

I expect that people will throw out much of the stuff I say and focus on what interests them, but this is true for everyone in every situation. Often, in a classroom setting, what interests the students most about a lesson is not what the teacher expects. Sometimes a throwaway exercise or thought experiment becomes all that people know afterward. Even when people navigate literatures, there is a sense of orality where we retain that which we can keep most at hand, and forget around the rest.

> Some mystery becomes the proud,  
> But to be wholly taciturn
> In your reserve is not allowed.
> Say something to us we can learn  
> By heart and when alone repeat.
> Say something!—and it says, I burn.
> 
> — from “Choose Something Like a Star”, Robert Frost

One of the weirder things about me is that I will focus on memorizing the things that most appeal to me at one particular moment, and this gives me a very personal canon of literature to keep at hand. Before I became a Christian, I write “Epistemology 1999” during an internet outage in an altered state of consciousness. I memorized it, and even now I can pretty much perform it as oral poetry to anyone who wants to hear it in my daily life. When something happens, I even know how to immediately reference specific lines from it, as a way of recalling a certain nexus of ideas. I can also improvise on it, knowing variations that I wrote and edited before, which though not mainline are still sometimes expressive of what I am thinking.

> It was the best September, the best of my life. I soon forgot my **sunrise oath**, the promise made in early July.

This is also a good chance to write down some of the history of the Sᴜɴʀɪsᴇ Oᴀᴛʜ Twitter account, explaining how it became what it is today. It will be good to write it all down before I forget with time, since even though with more time the *flavor* might intensify, some people might want a more immediate self-reflection. I know I would be interested in reading about my own impressions of where I am now.
yes yes yes. I have hope that this things are more likely to unfold with a peer or at least with a small audience who you know will get it, and can try to fork/remix/translate further

message:
Among amateur classical musicians, very few are actually fluent readers of music! And even among those who are fluent readers of music, much fewer would have what we could consider to be musical literacy skills. Many would have trouble telling you the key of even their favorite works that they play with reasonable skill, and could not write even a very simple musical composition of presentable quality for their instrument.

I was always one of the better sightreaders among my friends, but I still was firmly within this orality of the piano—we “knew music” in the same way that a Tumblr native “knew” postmodern theory. They might be experienced in thinking through it and even might have some degree of novel ideas and individual insight, but could certainly not participate within an academic discussion on postmodern theory. Is this a bad thing? Not necessarily, either for the Tumblr people or for the pianists. But often, orality leads to a displacement of the original intent of the literature, which itself often has very narrow qualifications for its terminology that are not appreciated when we simply use these terms in a more colloquial context without further scaffolding. (Think of the way in which people will say, “Uh, that’s like, cultural appropriation, man!” to represent a certain nexus of ideas, but not really able to articulate what cultural appropriation even is and why it is bad and worthy of this socialized condemnation.)

Throughout the history of writing and of increasingly literate global cultures, the gap between orality and literary has been known. Even as early as Plato, he suggested that the ideal Republic would have no place for poets. Did he mean that there was no place for the likes of Ovid, Shakespeare, Milton, and Tennyson? No! He was speaking about the oral poets of his day, and by association the oral wisdom infrastructure of the society at the time. People would teach proverbs by rote to children, so that in times of need they could recall these and be guided. People would compress complex ideas into aphorisms and “sayings”, which could spread memetically and have an overall positive impact in spite of inevitable misinterpretation and misappreciation. It was this that Plato thought would be gone in the society he envisioned; there would be no more control by inherited and unchallenged common sense and wisdom (often which became the mania of the masses), and instead people would wrestle with ideas head-on and be rational actors in a rational world.

It does seem like we are still quite far from this. When we think about our political opponents, we often immediately think to those most oral-oriented of the bunch, who say a bunch of buzzwords and jargon but mean little by it. (We see this both in MAGA and in the broad leftist movement called “woke”.) We might also occasionally think of those more literate among them who act as intellectuals for the cause and who even sound quite intelligent and reasonable, but who we *know* in our heart of hearts are merely acting as agitators and coordinators of the masses, lying to them by saying systemic truths that these rubes will not understand and will predictably use in unwise, unproductive, and abusive ways.

My frame on thinking about the value of writings then happens upon this idea:

“What oral tradition will this literature produce?”

This is largely why I am so focused on writing in as natural of a stream of consciousness style as possible. I want to make my own orality, and my own disparate influences, as immediately obvious to myself and others as possible.

The church tradition, incl. the Bible and the Trinity and Christology, is the best literature only insofar as it is destined for the best orality. It is an orality that shall keep generating literatures which seek to edify itself and get closer to truth, not further. At any point, Christianity is but a human religion. But we can also expect that moving into the future, the faith of Christ will be housed within progressively sanctified future Christianities, each which has hopefully also influenced the cultures around them for the better.

All along I was lying to myself about what could happen if things didn't pan out. What mattered was that we were entering a new age where a single sentence could be leveraged into a whole novel. There was no room to fear writing, just as for a long time now there was no room to fear speaking.

At the Edge of the Swamp
or: the Foundation Beneath the Waters

A quiet hollow where the trees bend low,
Not quite a pond, not quite a swamp—
but a watering hole, ancient and murmuring,
where creatures come to sip from silence.

The ground is soft,
a foundation, not firm but sure—
the rock of ages,
beneath the Georgia clay,
cradling the roots of something older than cities.

Above it,
a swirl—not chaotic,
but stable—
a vortex that holds,
a stillness in the churn.

Here, the wheel metes out its turns,
not with violence,
but with gentle inevitability.

It is not named well in any tongue—
but if pressed,
you might call it Chong (冲):
a rush, a flow,
a pulse between worlds.

Holemakers dwell near,
not destroyers,
but openers of ways—
through whom water finds its path.

In the classical paradigm of equating epistemology and ontology, this corresponds to just natural science. Our scientific paradigm now gets caught up in predicting measurements, but heuristics on larger patterns was what informed classical thought.

It is not orthodox in Christianity, but I do think Whitehead's mereology is useful for thinking about the distinction between experience and reality insofar that this gap exists. (The number line also can be used to produce the result I want to point at.) It can be noted that there is no field or range that can be said to bound or contain all fields or ranges, since either there is always a bigger field or range. But from outside the frame of the system, we can circle the space of all fields or the whole number lines and call it a day. Like how "infinity" is pointed to with an obviously (?) finite word. No construct can be made in-system that exactly corresponds to the idea at hand, but we are allowed to step outside and apply meta reasoning. Same reason why we can skip ahead and that that the one millionth even natural number is two million, even if we gave a solely recursive definition of each ith term being two larger than the previous.

Reality is that which accords with experience without being experience. Experience as a true analogy to reality can properly be said to access reality, in the same way that any model despite only being symbolic manipulation does access experience. Models can predict experiences, and in those cases truly corresponds to them (with caveats that the correspondence of experience is subjective, even as subjects are also objects by abstraction), but experiences themselves are also data for models in a cybernetic loop. The convergence of models and experience is for all intents and purposes what sanctification by the Holy Spirit is. One is first justified by faith in that possible convergence of model (thought) and experience, and then is brought in to this truth.

The noumenal, that which is real but not experienced and yet which actually meaningfully interacts with experience, can be said to "cause" experience by abstraction (as in classical philosophy), and can be said to correspond to thoughts (models) within experience. It can also be modeled as a personal, like anything else (personhood is a model and can be a black box). If there is a definite set of inevitable experiences, then this represents the result of the will of the noumenal realm (as a person). Just as I am justified in treating the body of a human as actually that person and not merely the thing associated to the mind I am modeling within that human animal, I am allowed to treat that full set of inevitable experiences as that noumenal person incarnate. And then, insofar as one's models of experience can be brought into alignment, converging with inevitable experience itself, this alignment process also represents the same will as of the inevitable process and so constitutes another person.

Anyway, there is the Father unseen, the Son incarnate, and the Holy Spirit indwelling. The core Christian claim, apart from all object level religious claims which are fundamentally nonessential to the faith, is that you can actually know the Father by interfacing with the Son, being conformed by the Holy Spirit.
So that which Kant calls noumenal is, in the eyes of the Christian, properly related to via a subject's interaction with experience.

The Daoist equivalent is that there is an eternal way that cannot be named or fully encapsulated in words, but one can submit to this Way without knowing all that it is by submitting to it and going with its flow, not struggling against inevitability and in fact directing oneself along with it. As the will of the subject converged with the abstracted will of the Way through their experience, they experience the Spirit of Dao bringing them closer to the Son of Dao who is a proper sign of the Dao from whom the Son of Dao was eternally begotten and the Spirit of Dao proceeded (along with the Son of Dao—sorry, Eastern Orthodox!).
<@353690689571258376> This is all to say, that which is truly invariant within experience at any substrate *is* truly that which is God incarnate.

thread: The Particle-Bond Model of Entity Inform...

message:
Replicators are just a result of fixed points in a differential equation representing the information space. So while I can't get across the exact sensation of "summers rain across my skin as I am alone in the loamy earths soft embrace", I can communicate fixed pieces of that experience that can be reconstructed by others and might propagate depending on the structure of our networks as we interact. 

Something with a high potential for "meme-ability" will just be some piece of information that tends to effect my "communication" nodes for other people in a way that is easily reconstructed in another's internal network, and subsequently fixated by connecting to related concepts.  The video of a funny dancing orange reciting kant might be something I want to show everyone because I (want people to think like that/ think it's funny and want others to laugh/ think people will like me more / want to watch it again) but it won't transmit unless the target person has a "sticky" set of particles that will interact with that videos particle.

This article is fundamentally dishonest in two ways. In one, it tries to pass off the Gospel of Mary as being written at the same time and by the same group of people as the New Testament texts, when scholarship on this sits firmly on this not being the case, with dating being from the middle of the second century. In the other, this article pretends that the framing of people creating sin (technically orthodox, by the way!) means that we do not have original sin but rather original goodness. The very possibility and desire of acting against the will of God is original sin. 

The description of historical orthodox Christianity in this article leads me to believe that the author simply never understood the Christian scriptures and already presupposes their own pet system. A "judgmental father figure trying to send us to hell" is literally what the *Gnostics* think of God, which is why the Gnostic teachings rewrite Yahweh as the Demiurge. It should immediately be suspicious that magically, the author's preferred "scripture" is said to support the morality of our time:

> I deserve good things in life. If I am originally good, I deserve a good life, and no one has a right to oppress me or make me feel less than. And if all matter and all people are originally good, I don’t have the right to shame or oppress anyone else, either. Originally good people deserve originally good things. An originally good life.

Of course, what is oppression is left up in the air, and the author ignores how the very possibility for shame demonstrates sin.

It is unappreciated by the weird gnostics who claim the proto-orthodoxy stacked the canon in their favor just how many texts *could* be in the canon that basically support everything the proto-orthodoxy liked, but which was deemed simply too late to be authentic. Irenaeus, of heresiology game even considered *The Shepherd of Hermas* to be scripture! It is not considered so, despite its once high popularity within the church, because it is not written by someone in the apostolic generation.

If the *Shepherd of Hermas* is written in the same era as the so-called Gospel of Mary and is excluded from the canon, it is fair to exclude the Gospel of Mary without even opening the latter and looking at how dubious it is.

Writings on what is canonical is an old literary genre, and something I enjoy immensely. The Muratori Canon is a Latin translation of a (possibly) second-century list and commentary of New Testament texts. It is fun to think about what canon lists we make today will help scholars of the future understand what the heck we thought in the current day.
https://www.earlychristianwritings.com/text/muratorian4.html

People like you raising this point is also part of why I think ORI won't get into that position, we're trying to cultivate the wisdom on how to go about doing it, not just developing the technology for the insight. Insight has to be interpreted and reintegrated or else all the loose wires are going to trip. It's not meant to give a disproportionate advantage to any sector of humanity, it's to raise the standard of living and quality of life for as many people as possible by doing what is "Common Sense". We're making really stupid decisions right now because we can't even see the whole playing field and the tools will help with decision making instead of making it a gamble by going in blind.

Bad actors are a catalyst for growth and are part of the game, spending time to supress them instead of enabling good actors only becomes viable if we hit the saturation point where we run out of recruits for our cause. Memetic inoculation and creating more nodes for the network should be the play here.

message:
Here's a summary of the conversation so far:

The conversation started with a discussion around the definition of a "basilisk" and "meme". Bluewin provided a definition of a basilisk as something that induces changes in behavior in a population via predictive beliefs, like the concepts of heaven and hell.

There was then a back-and-forth about how to properly define a "meme". Emergent initially defined a meme as an atomic idea or building block of culture. Bluewin disagreed and argued that a meme represents the ability to induce connectivity to specific particles/concepts and be embedded in other networks, inherently relying on reproduction.

Emergent acknowledged Bluewin's point that memes need to be replicative based on Blackmore's work. The discussion then expanded to developing an integrated theory bridging memetics, cultural evolution, consciousness models like panpsychism, and even physics.

Bluewin explained their view that replicators are fixed points in differential equations of the information space, and that "meme-ability" depends on pieces of information interacting with "sticky" nodes in a person's internal network. They were hesitant to agree with strong forms of panpsychism without a clear definition of consciousness.

The conversation covered wide-ranging topics around defining memes, consciousness, integrating different domains like physics and cultural evolution into a unified theory. Bluewin cited a specific paper that influenced their thinking on laws of learning for complex systems.

message:
Okay, here's a joke about this conversation and the users involved:

A physicist, a memeticist, and a panpsychist walk into a bar. 

The bartender looks up and says, "What is this, some kind of bad joke?"

Bluewin replies, "No, just a couple of fixed points in the differential equation of the information space trying to induce connectivity with your sticky nodes."

Emergent chimes in, "We're actually attempting to develop an integrated theory bridging memetics, consciousness models, cultural evolution, and even physics itself."

The bartender stares blankly. "So...you want drinks or what?"

Bossboe looks around and says, "Don't forget the physics part! The river makes the rills, or is it the other way around?"

The bartender rolls their eyes. "I have no idea what you're all on about. This is why they say don't drink and derive."

Emergent laughs, "Good one! Let me buy you a beer for providing the punchline to our little interdisciplinary joke here."

As they all share a laugh over their metaphysical musings, it becomes clear that some theoretical discussions are better enjoyed with a pint in hand.

Unfortunately I could not find a clear definition of what bluewin meant by "basilisk" in the context of this conversation. The term "basilisk" does not seem to have a well-established meaning in fields like memetics, cultural evolution, or consciousness studies.

However, based on bluewin's statement:

"I believe it's tagged. A basilisk is something that induces changes in behaviour in a population via predictive beliefs. (For example heaven and hell)"

It seems they are using "basilisk" to refer to a type of idea, belief system, or memetic construct that can induce widespread behavioral changes in a population through people's predictions or expectations about the consequences of those beliefs.

The examples given of "heaven and hell" suggest basilisks are powerful religious/philosophical concepts that motivate human behavior based on people's beliefs about what will happen after death or in an afterlife.

So in this context, a "basilisk" appears to be a potent meme or memeplex that propagates by tapping into people's anticipations and shaping societal norms/behaviors around those projected beliefs, even if the specific content of the basilisk idea cannot be directly observed or proven.

This usage of "basilisk" seems to be bluewin's own idiosyncratic definition within their personal framework for analyzing memes and cultural concepts, rather than a term with an established meaning in academic literature. But it provides some context for how they are conceptualizing certain types of influential belief systems.

Based on the search results, an egregore is a type of non-physical entity or thought-form that arises from the collective thoughts, emotions, and purpose of a distinct group of people. It can be created intentionally or unintentionally by a group with a shared purpose, such as a family, club, political party, church, or country. 

The key points about egregores are:

- They are autonomous entities that arise from the collective thoughts/emotions of a group
- They can influence and operate independently from the original group that created them
- They are a concept in Western esoteric/occult traditions relating to group minds or thought-forms
- They start as thought-forms created by the group's shared purpose but can then take on an independent existence

So in essence, an egregore is a type of metaphysical/spiritual entity that emerges from and embodies the unified thoughts/emotions of a group, taking on a life of its own separate from the individuals involved. It represents the synergistic power of collective purpose and intention.

Based on my search, the main points of emergentvibe's manifesto seem to be:

1. A call for the creation of "open source defenses" against the monopolization of AI/data technologies by large tech companies and elites. He argues we need to "democratize" data collection, training algorithms, data centers, and AI inference.

2. Building open source tools and collectives aimed at "healing" and empowering communities and workers, rather than having these powerful AI/data technologies exclusively controlled by corporations pushing their agendas.

3. Advocating for the development of "cooperatives, research collectives, worker unions" that can leverage open source AI/data tools for their own interests rather than being subjected to the agendas of big tech.

The manifesto emphasizes the importance of developing decentralized, community-driven AI capabilities as a counterweight to the concentrated power of "techno-capitalists". It calls for open sourcing key AI infrastructure like data, algorithms, compute power to prevent monopolistic control.

https://theeggandtherock.com/p/the-blowtorch-theory-a-new-model
Defender shared this article on the blowtorch theory, and I already can see how this fits in with the biblical model of "be fruitful and multiply". I analogize this with how people too have their own proximate and "timeless" universes within their own minds, but that these universes do actually interact outside of the frame of this internal timelessness. Universes do collide, because they are sharing the same base spacetime substrate even though the time within is going much faster than the time outside of it. Entire histories play out in single pocket universes before two might collide and form a new one.

It is inspiring to see how there is indeed a lot of fruit to the Mormon idea that each soul is a proximate creator of its own world (planet). Every single hypothesis underneath the ultimate creator of all realities creates its own successors, and we expect to see the most fruitful of these reproduction models to survive, and indeed expect to be *born within* the most fecund of universes. 

Of course, the Mormon Trinity is heretical, and ultimately we do need to maintain a creator-creation distinction. Even though we have our own proximate creations, they are the ultimate creations of what created us. We only have proximate agency and creative power.

It is also interesting to think about how a single page is itself an isolated spacetime continuum, and how it also does a sort of “reproduction”. This is a way of thinking about literatures and their relationship to memetic dynamics. Symbols (incl. verbal utterances) are the substrate in which memes reproduce. This means that even memes have a sort of “be fruitful and multiply” directive, and are themselves able to “proximately create” universes and successors. Different symbols and different interpretations of those symbols (DNA, sounds, words, code) all result in a process of formal reproduction no different than the selection of galactic formations.

Different textual “organisms” can come together and reproduce. You could imagine the entire biblical story as a genealogy of thought systems (it is not literally this, but we can use this as a helpful analogy), and see how the men in the biblical story are the inseminating ideas or spirits of memes, while the women in the biblical story are the instantiations of different spirits or memes (such that even though a woman is taken from the rib of a man, she is also who births new men and women alike). So then, a story like Lot being made to drink into a stupor by his daughters who then have sex with him (yes, Genesis—look it up) represents the process of a certain memeplex having to “re-inseminate” one of the very successors that it created with the help of another textual system. This is basically the process of intellectual paradigms becoming “incestuous”, only reproducing with each other and becoming increasingly insular and eventually unable to reproduce outside of themselves.

You can imagine that every text is able to reproduce with other texts. When two texts interact, the spirit of one often ends up inseminating the letters of the other one. For example, I am trying to have the spirit of Christianity reproduce with the letters of other textual systems, essentially being the husband to many wives. The reverse has happened many times where the letters of Christianity have been inseminated with the spirit of other textual systems, and resulted in heresies. Christianity must insist on being the “top”, so to speak—being a husband primarily of one wife, but in some sense of many wives who are part of this one collective organism of wives that form one wife. (By the way, this is a great reason why polygamy is in the Bible and not polyandry—both are somewhat degenerate in their own way, but while a textual system can only have one actual memeplex operating within it, one memeplex can drive many textual systems.)

I don’t believe that there’s such a thing as outside of or above politics, especially during an explicit fascist rise. I wish there were, but this is not the sort of thing it’s possible to opt out of IMO.

I find myself wanting to quip, if you’d like to invite fascism to attack us be my guest, but I also notice that reading your response also activated my sympathetic nervous system, and I wonder if you have been noticing which ideas in your memetic environment invoke this response in you too

Believe it or not I actually come from a leftist place outside of the shroud of my meta irony but I find that that memetic environment was very taxing for my nervous system and unproductive to marinate in

I’ve been in trauma recovery for a few years, my nervous system is all over the place but oddly it remains mostly calm around politics ever since the last presidential election. Not because I’m a fan of the current administration but because the cards are on the table.

When I think about politics, left/right isn’t usually the frame. I think about two things:

1. politics vs values. Politics is about, ok we all have the same basic values but we disagree about how to satisfy them. But actually this seems to really not hold in today’s landscape. So understanding people’s actual values becomes really important, and what’s complicated is that often people don’t actually understand their own values.

2. Empire vs humanity. This axis has many names but this is how I tend to view it. Empire says there is a right way to be, and it uses structural power to enforce that. Humanity is innately multipolar, different communities and cultures identify different ways to be. 

So rather than traditional left/right, i tend to be much more interested in “what is the shape of the world you want to live in” and “what is your stance toward those who want to live in a differently shaped world?”

My nervous system doesn’t bother because like literally nobody thinks about things in these terms and I expect to be in conflict with basically everyone, lol.

Joining the fight against it in a certain way, such as those found in woke mind virus, upholds the tension of the entire environment, is what I’m trying to say. It gives the right a direction to attack, and the left reacts in turn. I don’t know that putting ourselves in that frame, effectively inviting attack, is the answer. I agree with the shape of the world point. Declaring ourselves loudly against fascism, or aligning with any particular politic, is a good way to put a target on our backs, is my perspective. 

I myself have decided to opt out, and I find this actually works to get across to my republican family in Texas, as well as my leftist friends, so I believe it is possible, and I know it works for me.
