<@1353702929034514453> summarise the view points of each individual in this conversation and identify the main points of disagreement.

Here's a summary of the key viewpoints and disagreements expressed in the conversation:

Myk's main concern is about preventing bad actors and fascists from potentially co-opting or misusing any powerful tools/insights developed by the group for malicious purposes targeting people. He questions what defenses are in place against this.

Defender argues that the tools themselves are neutral, and it comes down to who people put their trust in to use the tools responsibly. He believes making the tools decentralized and available to communities can counteract centralized power/platforms. However, he acknowledges the need to plan for potential appropriation.

Metasyn takes the stance that tools are meant to enable humanity, and both good and bad actors will use them according to their values. He sees bad actors as catalysts for growth that can be modeled and doesn't advocate suppressing them over enabling good actors. Building more nodes in the network is the play.

Bradie suggests positioning the group as outside of or above politics to avoid becoming a target. He believes aligning overtly against fascism or with a particular politic invites attack. Instead, stabilizing in an integrative headspace that draws people in makes the group harder to weaponize.

The key disagreement seems to be around how explicitly to take a political stance against fascist ideologies versus attempting to remain above the political fray. Some see overtly opposing fascism as necessary, while others believe that makes the group too much of a target and favors a more integrative, apolitical framing.

Yes, complete conversion is a form of victory. In the end, anyone who is opposed to the identities associated with victory rather than conformed to them will experience the defeat. If everyone converted to the winning side, everyone would win, but that old identity would die.

But this dying out *would* include people literally dying out in the case where they prefer the old identity over the actual truth of how things are. Imagine a case where someone rejects modern medicine that could save them from their illness. Or, imagine being the one person who thinks that he specifically going around doing whatever he wants (esp. of a violent nature) is the way that things should be. That identity will either die with those holding the identity having converted, or it will die with the people who latch onto that identity til the end. In this sense, there is necessarily sin, and repentance, and hell, and heaven.
What happens when the man who thinks he can do what he wants is the one with an army to command

If he actually can do whatever he wants to the very end, this is equivalent to saying that God is on his side. Like, if he literally is provably going to heaven and taking a seat next to God after achieving victory with his army, he just *is* the chosen one, right?

Ultimately, might makes right. The real question is what is this true might. What humans think is might is not ultimately mighty. Empires fall despite their swords being bigger and their files being deeper.
I know that in doing what I want, until the very end, the war machine will die by my might
Depends on what doing what you want means! If it is also what God wants, then yeah, you win. Congrats. God is the ultimate victor, tautologically.

But part of "what happens" is not just that ultimate victory by God, but also the story along the way that includes many other things which we may or may not like, and may or may not accept with open hearts. Those moments reveal our sin. But our sins are forgiven through God allowing us to identify in him.
I see sin every step I take in this world, in the food I eat, in the clothes I wear, in the shampoo I use, in my toothpaste

Every action we take is corrupted by our sin, and by the sin of the world of men as it is, and by the evils that the powers and principality perpetuate. Yet, God ultimately intends those *same actions* for good. This is where we are challenged.
Sin is not stopped by actions being prevented, but by hearts being aligned. Everything that happens now and forever is what God wants, but is it what we want?

Characters all do exactly what the author wants, but for their own fallen reasons. The ones who perfectly want what the author wants are truly the author incarnated within the story.

We could say that. Or we could say that there is already that which is going to happen, and a soul that is meant to carry out what happens either is accorded to it or they are not. We take the actions we take and think the thoughts we have, but how we react to it, how we react to it, is our assurance to ourselves of whether we are actually among those who God chose and who love God.
We do not have ultimate certainty over the state of souls, but we are allowed our assurance.

Those who love that which is doomed to die, proximately at the hands of men and ultimately at the hands of God, are not part of victory.

It is no coincidence that those who love their man-lying-with-man-as-with-woman sin largely hate God. (Not all! There are homosexual Christians.) Gracefully, God has brought homosexuals closer and closer to him over the ages, and this will not stop.

When I speak of God being on my side, it is only ever in the hope that I am walking in love, and tending life. I have no interest in claiming victory, that is for God to claim. I am here to keep the garden healthy.
Who defines love? I place the definition of love in the eyes of God. Love is life. Homosexuality is dead. It cannot itself be love even when it is a part of love.
You mistake your definition with God’s
God is victory. If your idea of a god is one that can lose, it is a useless god.
If I am wrong about homosexuals, then I hope that homosexuals abound on this earth and let their fruits multiply to the ends of the earth as God wants.
And that would be so beautiful!
Amen
I agree, it is beautiful when God wins. I am not dictating God's victory. I am trying to describe it to the best of my *fallible* ability.

If I am right about homosexuals, then I hope that homosexuals are pounded in the dust just like child sacrificers and demon worshippers were, and that their tree is cursed and left to die as God wants.
Either way, I love what God will have to say and will stand by the decision of God.
Either I am right about the decision of God, or I am wrong. If I am wrong, I shall repent. But I have the assurance that I am right. For now.
I have come to know the garden and I trust it to hold strong
Note that no one disagrees on the fact that the homosexuals which the Bible was centrally talking about, a.k.a. pederasters and rapists, were pounded into the dust.
A good gardener knows when to let the soil lie
I think my point was made
I too, know war
If we are on the same side, we win and lose together. If we are on different sides, either one will win and the other will lose, or God will show us that we were on the same side.

Even more interesting to me is if we could track retroactively the behavior of 4chan to see if we could identify the point at which the feds took control of it by looking at how its memetic footprint changed.
They would allow us to identify similar changes in other communities.
It does feel nice when the men patrol the perimeters to protect the garden from looters and thieves
No one feels bad for the ones who sacrificed children to demons. No one feels bad for the pederasts. No one will feel bad for the sinners of this age.
You’re sitting here equating queer people to murderers and pederasts with zero self-awareness and it’s pretty gross, dude, ngl.

By what standard can I not make this equivalence? Would you be as concerned if I equated rapists and thieves and unfair masters to murderers and pederasts? Seems like you also did not comment on the seeingly more mild sin of mixed fabrics and pork.

Trust me on this when I say I likely have much more self-awareness than you do. I am saying the unpopular opinion. I am saying the opinion that could cause me to lose a job. I am saying the opinion that practically everyone here is going to disagree with. Do you think I am naive, and am saying this without any inkling of the fact that some people are going to see my words a certain way? It is I who know what is at stake when I say this, and know exactly what kind of conclusions you will draw about me, because I know how the world thinks about sin and about sexual norms. It seems to be you who lack the awareness of where you are standing in this conversation.

Are you standing against the so-called empire you say you hate, or are you its representative? You are standing on the side of a crushing popular opinion, against a viewpoint that perhaps by your own assessment is on its way out. I of course am well aware I am speaking from the side of power in eternity. But you who are so certain of your own victory, why do you disgrace yourself as the victor you think you are? Answer.
I am quite hungry to mine the tension for information in support of my frame

Sin is sin. The future shall select against those who identify in their sin, whether it is white lies or eating pork or wearing mixed fabrics or swearing or philandering or raping or murdering or having gay sex or kneeling in front of golden calfs. Consider this a scientific theory on sociology, one which I have credited in advance to my theory that the Bible is truly the Word of God. What is there to fear, if I am on the side of defeat? Wait and see.

I am as beholden to God for my sins as anyone else. 🙂 I am also among those who deserve death. The only salvation is to identify in the source of all life, and repent from the ways of the kingdom of the fallen.

I don’t hate, dude. Not even empire. I’m just sad. There’s nothing I can say to you that will change your mind, and I’m not really interested in trying.

All this is reducible to you to objective claims and power as measured in terms of domination and that entire frame feels completely wrong to me, in ways that cause harm, and in ways that I don’t think can be articulated to the satisfaction of someone who is committed to that frame.

I just feel like life doesn’t have to be this constant power game with deadly stakes, and wish people would just chill.

Life *is* a constant power game with deadly stakes, and we have already lost. You are going to biologically die. I am going to biologically die. I have good reason to believe this. I know that some things will persist longer than others after our biological death, though all shall eventually die as we know it unless it is that which never dies.

There is much that can be shown to me to change my mind, but the world has not shown it. If the world had a flourishing nation under the rule and citizenry of those living in what I call sin, then that would change my mind! America had a chance to prove this, along with Canada! Instead, we got exactly the same degeneracy that was warned about.

It is *because* I already lost the game and can rely on God for my salvation that I can be chill 🙂 I could do nothing, then wait and see. But I also want to tell you what I see how, with a fresh pair of eyes. I am someone who grew up without any reason whatsoever to hate homosexuals, and indeed both had homosexual urges and was free to act on them. Yet I knew it was disgusting and disgraceful. This was innate, despite being by all accounts liberal and accepting. I understand now that my instinct was not wrong, but rather my upbringing.

I brought up the sexual ethics question at a book study with a super liberal study group, with a pastor named Aaron Miller who was a little less liberal but who had written a book, *Witnesses Of These Things*, about how my denomination (the United Church of Canada) can serve in these changing times. The answer he gave, I actually agree with! Basically, in short, theological liberals should study the Bible more so they have real arguments against theological conservatives that are not heretical (like “The Bible is not a divinely inspired document”). He even gave a pretty good defense on the fraught territory of discerning whether sexual and gender minorities are living in sin, given the limits of our understanding of the mind of God. He even used the example of Genesis 1 to show how while people do not strictly demarcate between day and night, land and sea, etc., they are tempted to do so with gender. (I actually disagree with his exegesis here, but it was interesting food for thought.)

Nothing in the Bible says that a queer person is not axiomatically fine. Rather, it makes a prediction of the outcomes of particular actions. *If* queer people are more likely to take those actions, they are more likely to suffer those consequences.

I would not be saying that teenage boys are not axiomatically fine if I say that shoplifting and premarital sex has negative temporal (and potentially eternal) consequences. I would be saying that they by being prone to committing those particular sins makes them suffer more of those predicted consequences.

If the consequences act as a selection effect, then we should either expect a group who experiences those consequences more to either be selected out, *or* to change such that they do not suffer those consequences.

The genre of Christian who wants a reason to beat their gay kids over the Bible to make them become straight, but also does not care if these kids have straight premarital sex… obviously not what I support. They too are living in sin, and so are being selected against. But wait, am I saying they are ontologically not fine by their identities outside of their identity with their sin? No. Not at all. But insofar that they identify in sin, and this sin *is* selected against, they shall experience a walk outside of God’s grace.

<@1221182134950301706> This takes me back, I miss those days 🙂 (Went looking for our former exchange with <@752744975456010291> on an ontology of sin as unskill, and found a lot of old goodies from before a bunch of new people got added.)

After had a certain mystic experience that could only be described as accessing the Akashic Records and reaching the Root, I liberally used this language before I became more intimately familiar with Christian views. It was essentially something like, an explosion of meaning making, and an intimate awareness of sign and significance.

Overall, I think that I probably could have picked a less controversial example to stake my ground on. Would have been more strategic, maybe. But I also think that these truths are *necessary* to say.

The position of Myk and Bradie is that offering a framework of sexual ethics which treats man-lying-with-man-as-with-woman as a sin—in the Christian sense of cause for separation from God, temporal consequences, and ultimately eternal damnation—is prima facie homophobic or intolerant. I strongly press against this, and also want to specifically call out how they are intent on poisoning the well for this discussion to associate views with me that I do not support, incl. (1) gay people are ontologically bad and (2) man-lying-with-man-as-with-woman is equal to murder and rape in the contexts of legal sanction or norm-setting. I *do* unapologetically support the view that man-lying-with-man-as-with-woman is equal with murder and rape in terms of the ontological status of being sin, same as dishonesty and blasphemy and everything in between. But this is hardly a controversial view among Christians. All have fallen short.

Why did I bring up sexual ethics? Again, because literally none of you would disagree with me if I brought up, say, murder and rape as sins which if not repented from result in eternal damnation. I brought up sexual ethics because it is one of the *most* common stumbling blocks right now, both for non-Christians *and* for Christians. It is also an important area for understanding my systems view of sin. Much of my own experimental work in the church relates to how theological liberals and theological conservatives talk about this issue.

I know that Myk and Bradie have expressed discomfort at the idea that this view and its expression can be sanctioned within the Open Research Institute (ORI). Frankly, I express equal discomfort at the idea that this view and its expression *cannot* be sanctioned within the Open Research Institute (ORI). I am explicitly here to further the Christian project, and to fulfill what I consider to be a central ideal in Acts 2 of translating the gospel into tongues (languages, systems) that people understand. If the orthodox traditional historical Christian faith does not have a place here, I do not have a place here. It is as simple as that.

I will also offer the interesting systems observation (or maybe just intuition) that those who intuitively support sin (or who believe in some kind of baseline equality of lifestyle and worldview in which no selection effects happen without the appearance of Big Bad Men performing selection themselves out of nothing more than bigotry) tend to be the type who want to shut down conversation and perform the same characteristic well-poisoning that we observe here. They feel justified in it, of course, due to the Pelagian view that humans were born fundamentally good, and so we should just get out of the way for people to be naturally good, and calling some actions bad is therefore hateful to those for whom those actions are most natural and endemic. Sin begets sin; there is the actual sin, then there is sin identity, then there is meta–sin-identity where people start to identify with those who identify in the sin. This is where I assess Myk and Bradie of being. They may or may not partake in the sin themselves, but in service of the people who identify in the sin, they must run interference for what they consider to be the greater good.

This is where I kinda have to say “Told you so!” with regards to the thoughts on gatekeeping and initial “chain of command” or “laying of hands” dynamics that I mentioned are important for these types of groups. The early church had to drive out heretics who distorted the gospel (sorry, gnostics and Arians!), and I see that this group is going to have its own Council of Nicaea sooner or other to actually set its central mission straight.

I accuse Myk and Bradie of entryism, of coming in with the intention of changing the focus of the space from what it was. Perhaps this is an accusation that only makes sense from my perspective, as I joined this group (was the first member of this Discord other than Defender!) with the understanding (one-sided or not) that this would be for creating a public literature and generating an open research corpus with many associated technologies. I have, of course, made it clear that I merely think of this group as one chapter of a larger project, and that this chapter is under the effective dictatorship of its founder.

No part of gay culture *requires* the corruption of the marital act. Indeed, many homosexuals across history have furthered humanity and God's plan, and I already gave a model for the many ways in which ancient gay culture (with endemic pederasty) has been redeemed coming into the current day. It very well could be that these gay cultures can keep on existing yet no longer be centered around, well, man-lying-with-man-as-with-woman. The memetic lineage of homosexuals, both within the gay community and through general cultural transmission, does not structurally need the act that scripture calls abomination.
thread: I mean, gay found families and gay

message: There are cultures that arise from many things of varying levels of potential and actual sin, and those cultures often have continued while reforming many of their activities. As a fun example, many crime families turn legit. I have the full expectation that historical gay culture (continuous or temporally recurrent, like a zombie), as something that *has* been increasingly sanctified over time, will continue to be sanctified. Either the desire will no longer exist, or the desire will not be acted on as it is acted on now. This is my prediction for the future.
thread: I mean, gay found families and gay

message: To the degree that the corruption of the marital act is centrally important to the gay community, they shall reap the bad fruits of this sin (I think maybe the most neutral comparison is just to drug abuse, honestly) and *also* both reap the good fruits of their other good living while also enjoying the temporal common grace of God within society. All are only eternally delivered from damnation through faith in Christ Jesus, so for me the question is mostly about whether or not gay people get into a position where they can put their trust in Jesus. When the Old Testament issues the death penalty to those who commit man-lying-with-man-as-with-woman, it is a witness to what predictably happens to this person’s legacy and to their eternal soul. *If* someone who commits this sin is *actually* among the elect who are aligned to God and identify in (put their faith in) Jesus, that is awesome, and they shall continue on the path of sanctification! But scripture tells us the *ordinary* situation, and the assurance of a Christian is based on these ordinary situations and means *without* arrogating the ability to dictate that God cannot work in other ways, esp. (and most pertinently here) in using sinners to do his good works, or calling people to him who we would not expect on the basis of assurance.

message:
Hi everyone 🙂 I made https://bluesky-sentiment-analysis.fly.dev/

It takes the bluesky firehose and performs some basic live sentiment analysis using the NRC Emotion Lexicon. https://saifmohammad.com/WebPages/NRC-Emotion-Lexicon.htm

next steps:

1. expand the lexicon: the lexicon consists of only 1400 words, each ranked in 10 dimensions. I plan to expand the lexicon with LLM generated data, in both total words and dimensions. I believe I can add dimensions like "belief" "left-lean" "right-lean".
2. I want to add a grammar field (memeome) visualisation. Perform frequency analysis and see the collective grammar field shifting over time. Automatically detect popular words and amend our lexicon.
3. Integrate with the twitter firehose provided by the Community Archive extension
4. Implement spike/deviation detection - when change in the collective memome is detected, have a bot post on bluesky/twitter about it
5. Create LLM summaries for each dimension, summarising the last 1h of posts in each category, to give us a sense of what topics may be causing spikes. 
6. Filter by likes/reposts, see how popularity affects the scores, perhaps generate embeddings for viral posts and plot them in a self organising map.
7. Create custom bluesky feeds - filter the top ranked tweets in each dimension into seperate bluesky feeds. e.g. Anger feed, Joy feed etc.
8. Add more languages - currently only supports english but the NRC has 110 languages
9. Create a heatmap for each dimension, assuming language=location
10. Create an open API to provide easy access to the live sentiment data
11. Create explainer page with a donation link to cover server costs 🙂

Questions:
- What are some dimensions you'd like to see explored?
- What are some corpuses of words you'd like to see added? (e.g. philosophy, psychology, tech, spirituality)?

github link: https://github.com/emergentvibe/bluesky-sentiment-analysis

To be clear, I fully plan on staking out this channel til I am asked to leave or rules are made that seek to prevent discussion of my own theological interests (most pertinently around sin and governance, given its close overlap with the culture war).

In my eyes, the easiest integration is that we establish baseline norms around the projection of intent and representation of opposing ideas in discourse. Reading the history of the chat in retrospect, things only got bitter or frictional after Bradie made a point of performing her disagreement (uncharitably, too, mind you), and then joining up with Myk to make broad assertions about what I feel about gay people.

By the way, here is my understanding of the other side of this discussion, written in the form of points which are not actually at odds with the fact that man-lying-with-man-as-with-woman is explicitly outlined as a sin in the scriptures:

1. Gay and queer people (and all sexual and gender minorities in general) largely have the desires and identities they do because of the conditions of their birth (both genetic and memetic, with little way of changing either) and of the social dynamics that naturally reify dynamics—with isolation from heteronormative spheres being one of the most predictable ones. 
2. Though often these gay and queer people cannot genetically procreate via sexual reproduction (though some can, and some do!), they still often carry on memetic lines that have profound *positive* cultural impact. 
3. Fruit is more expansive than simply sexual reproduction, though genetic fruit is largely on the basis of sexual reproduction. Even then, genetic lines are often preserved through both memetic contributions and from social cohesions with those who might not reproduce themselves. (This is why sterile heterosexual sex within marriage is actually still considered good in historical Christianity; it reinforces the proper marital act, which overall reifies the importance of genetic reproduction.)
4. We ought to be able to appreciate fruit as it is without reference to bad fruit (or lack of fruit) from the same tree. We ought not throw out the baby with the bathwater, in other words. An identity often has its own specific and particular good fruit which is not excluded by bad fruit.
5. Diversity is largely good and allows for better exploration of the complete spaces of both the genetic and memetic trees, thereby increasing overall fitness of all Man.

6. Legal and social censure on the basis of *immutable characteristics* (and not on the basis of *behavior*) tends to decrease diversity in a *negative* way.
7. Sanctification and social progress comes from eliminating bad fruit while promoting and preserving good fruit, not from solely culling every tree that plausibly has bad fruit.
8. The historical Christian church has often persecuted those who we call gay and queer individuals—often on the basis of *good* cause (pederasty and rape are bad), but significantly also on the basis of identity alone outside of behavior. 
9. Just because behaviors might lead to worse personal outcomes for the individual involved does not mean that everyone else should pitch in to make those personal outcomes worse and so eliminate them from the pool.
10. More net fruit is good, and we ought to seek the actual complete way, truth, and life—whatever it is.

At one point I was thinking of writing a whole essay on the issue of sexual ethics with every sentence only saying, “I am *not* saying…”, just so that we can avoid all the usual caltrops that come into this discussion where talking about behavior (and yes, man-lying-with-man-as-with-woman is a *behavior*, not an *immutable characteristic*) being more or less fit or optimal.

The two most pertinent comparisons is that either I am a doctor giving a diagnosis, or a football coach giving a game plan to a team. We can have a lot of discussion on how the suggestions and frameworks of a quack doctor or a football coach can have negative externalities. But both are centrally about behaviors and outcomes. In the case of the doctor, he does not *wish* for the illness that is being diagnosed, but is rather *describing* his model of it. In the case of the football coach, he is offering a model of “winning” behavior at the level of the game.

Again, I am not perfect or infallible. I of course do not know everything about the nature of sin in the eyes of God, and I also do not know the full scope of the game at hand. I just *do* know that in the abstract, certain strategies propagate and certain other strategies do not, given *any* selection criteria. (None of us would disagree that it is fine that some behaviors are selected out of the genetic and memetic pools.)

My view is that the identity which we now call gay or queer has historically been degenerate, and so has already experienced extinction many times, with each revival (due to natural genetic and memetic circumstances) being progressively sanctified. We are reaching one of the first points in history where communities largely centered around what Christians call sexual immorality are net positive enough for society that it stands to gain from supporting them or at least leaving them alone. However, as Myk himself indicated, these networks are *not* able to be completely isolated. To the extent that sexual and gender minorities cause negative externalities—for example, by eroding productive social norms and thus pillaging the commons of all network effects—they are projecting bad fruit that ought not be ignored simply because it is hard to quantify. (We equally ought not downgrade the harm that heteronormative society inflicts on individuals just because it is hard to quantify.)

It has been said that those who note the practical selection pressures acting against those who perform man-lying-with-man-as-with-woman are simply axiomatically presuming that the gay identity is ontologically invalid. Most important to note first is that the identity which we call “gay” or “homosexual” is a modern phenomenon, and is not something that has historically existed as such. After all, the reification of biological sex and sociological gender is extremely recent. (This is, I will note, broadly agreed upon among academics and is utterly uncontroversial when cited in other discussions.) Following that, I must criticize the silent assumption that just because no identity is ontologically invalid, this means that all identities ought be treated the same or should be expected to propagate equally. A significant example is that even Bradie and Myk, who speak about the importance of diversity, are more than happy to call for the elimination of what they consider bad trees with bad fruit—namely, me—on the basis of *behavior*. It is not actually disputed that discerning on the basis of *behavior* is valid, and they would correctly laugh in my face if I suggested that what they were doing is discriminating against my identity as a Christian. Of course they are not; they are looking not at identity, but behavior and their model of whether that behavior leads to good or bad fruit. This is exactly the same basis on which I can say that gay people as an identity are valid, but it is also not only permissible but even advisable to institute norms *or* at least offer the ethical advice to not engage in man-lying-with-man-as-with-woman.

On the subject of drugs, I offer the example of the *meth gambit*. For any short term utility horizon, it seems that doing drugs is better than doing anything else. The raw amount of pleasure from the chemical reaction with one’s neurochemistry greatly outweighs the pleasure one can easily get from something else, and the mental states into which one can enter can even be profoundly meaningful. Yet, we offer the common advice to, uh, not do drugs. We also mostly (though some people differ on this) tell people who are already doing drugs to go to rehabilitation centers, and in the case of those who are happy with their addiction and identified with it, who even have their own communities around it, mostly leave them to their own devices and properly sanction against them insofar as they disrupt the social fabric. If we can understand the difference in subjective utility between the inner view of one and the outer view of a macroorganism society on the subject of drugs, then we can also understand how we can properly say, without additional qualification but not to the exclusion of contextual specification, why it can be said that man-lying-with-man-as-with-woman is dispreferable.

Even to people who have the natural inclination to partake in drugs (commonly alcohol), we offer them the strong advice to not to drugs. We ought not deliberately *shame* them into this state, of course! And it should be earnestly about outcomes, societal duty, and witness rather than about the feeling of disgust (even though, I will note, the feeling of disgust itself is useful information for the underlying systems at play which we cannot rationalize about). But we would *not* say that we are abusing or mistreating people by instituting norms against drug use, despite the fact that those who then partake in drugs might then by nature of being outside of norms and within an apparently hostile memetic environment would then feel shame.

(I could also replace drug use here with crimes and other socially sanctioned actions, but that would be too loaded, I guess? Even though it applies no less. People feel shame from wanting to litter or actually littering within memetic environments, but we still feel that it is good to institute norms against littering.)

RE: disgust, it is also useful to apply this to the feeling of wrath. Acting solely to satisfy one’s wrath is sinful, but wrath is appropriately the instinct that informs us when we create systems of justice. It is the information that lets us even hypothesize in the first place that a particular action was wrong or that some kind of retribution, restitution, or rehabilitation (otherwise destruction of the sin) is necessary.

Everything I am saying here is utterly old news to those who have read my writings before, and to those who have thought harder about systems than me. However, it is also the exact train of thought that is unfamiliar to those who do not like engaging with supposedly harmful norms. So sometimes, the obvious needs to be said. Norms themselves underwent selection. If it can be said that divergent and uncommon desires and instincts have not been selected out for systemic reasons—and I completely agree with this idea, by the way—it can also be said that the widespread worldwide norms against the non-heterosexual marital act and gay or queer community identification in general were selected for for completely adaptive reasons.

I had this thought experiment for a stereotype experiment regarding a deck of cards, where people see subsets of the cards but each see a different weighting of the suits and ranks inside. They then have to make predictions about a totally fair deck.
This simulates the process of developing heuristics and probability models that become stereotypes based on anticipated experience, regardless of if they apply to the entire seed population.
Basically the model not having enough information and being biased in one direction through non consensual manipulation

I want to imagine a situation where people “rationally” know that the cards of each suit are just as likely to be the same rank (except they are not due to the rigging), but then naturally develop stereotypes that give them a gut instinct which brings them closer.

The people in the experiment are told that the deck is fair, and then are shown cards from the rigged deck, then are asked to predict. I posit that more naturally racist people will make better predictions.

Language redesign, not in the words themselves, more like designing the primitives to enhance interpretability
2-3 chunks of information at once
The growth of knowledge means we can't know everything, but we should be able to know anything.
Pay (time) as you play (make progress)
Represent knowledge as a network of concepts instead of linear text
Offload working memory by externalising relationships
Explore complexities without holding everything in mind

Organise knowledge by phenomena
Break knowledge into small, interconnected modules centred on questions or phenomena, not disciplines

Universal Semantic Primitives
Natural Semantic Metalanguage (NSM), which reduces concepts to 60-70ish core primitives
It's like scrollable text
Dictionary like

Turn knowledge exploration into a game with rewards for connecting ideas or solving problems. (infinite craft?)
Deliver knowledge in small, context-relevant doses rather than overwhelming dumps.

Minimal set of mental models (Tables, trees, Boxes, lines)
reusing familiar mental models
Map abstract concepts to familiar, concrete imagery

Aligns with cognitive strengths in problem-solving and narrative
toolkit of universal principles
Taxonomy Pruning: Regularly simplify and consolidate terminology, merging redundant or overly specialised terms
Semantic Compression: Develop a standardised, minimalist vocabulary for cross-domain communication, like a knowledge Esperanto.

Academic and professional communities are tied to disciplinary identities and jargon, which can resist unified or simplified approaches.

Problems with research

Different types of items and their roles
[02:08]
I publish a figure on a sample that I got
[02:11]
The person who made the sample is tagged under it and the method they used to make it is as well. 
The one who ran the experiment and the protocol they used. 
The one who analyzed the data and how
The one who made the figure how 
The one who made the contract outlining the experiments first
[02:12]
Then you have "stories"
[02:14]
Stories are generated by people selecting data and information from different sources and building cohesive "stories" citing the data

Ideally the citation in the paper would be clickable to bring up the data directly.
[02:15]
You could even build little libraries with different figures you collected together
[02:16]
To find them you could search by:
Method
Sample
Data type
Name
Instrument
Author
[02:17]
The stories would naturally be fed by the creators into bounties to generate proposals for other people to run experiments
[02:18]
A natural progression of the grant cycle involving competent ideas and natural collaborative action.

1. A tool that allows the formalization of scientific collaborations through smart contracts
2. The ability to post methods/samples and request collaborations based on what parts you need help with (sample search vs techniques search)
3. The ability to analyze data in repeated fashions using a free library of analysis and experimental techniques that are stored on and run on servers owned by the company (cloud flow)
4. The ability to include corporate infrastructure for running samples (TA instruments will run samples for a nominal fee/genetic testing/etc.) In the website
5. Organized accounting of expenses and the like for easy uploading to grants as well as keeping track of collaborator expenses to maintain fairness. 
6. The ability to interface with grant infrastructure as closely as possible (exporting all financial documents in formats they like etc.) 
7. Laboratory notebooks (hopefully with built in cloud flow)
8. Tracking of originality of ideas and methods directly and transparently 
Benefits: 
   Social - the ability to make access to science easier for underrepresented groups who normally could not access certain techniques or spaces. (Eg. A Slovenian chemistry student who couldn't get within 100 miles of a synchrotron who has an amazing experimental idea)
The a space where people can comment directly on collaboration experiences with individuals increasing personal accountability. 
The ability to track scientific contributions to collaborations so instead of just names on a paper we are aware of who actually does what work and can make informed decisions on competency.
A primacy marker on specific scientific ideas, proof that you came up with an idea of methods that is tagged to your specific profile lessening the risk of being scooped (although this will require moderator intervention definitely) 
Scientific - 
Standardization of methods and analysis inside the tool.
Methods written before publishing to allow space for negative results that won't get buried due to them not being exciting. 
A forum to discuss scientific results openly.
The gradual reduction in need for journals to publish in as the tool generates it's own metrics for scientific competency. 

Risks -
Not enough interested scientists.
Lack of trust in the metrics on the platform
Lack of trust in the ability to use the platform openly without getting scooped
Violation of contracts  without proper recourse
Low interest in usage of on platform analytical tools 
Scams, identity theft, etc. 
Solutions? 
Tap network of known scientists and use social skills to expand it further. Use the Gary net. 
Maybe generate metrics for collaborativeness, experimentalist, theoretician, sample generation, instrumentation, field data collection, writing, figure building. 
Make a firm contract upon viewing/joining about use of information from the posted research projects without acknowledgement (this also helps with the reproducibility problem) 
Legal shit (ask carbon?)
.edu domains and identity verification maybe?

1 and 2 : indirect (Facebook, research gate, Twitter) 
3 and 7: benchling (lab note book, tools only for molecular biology and simple excel sheets), electronic notebooks, technique wikis
8: electronic notebooks and journals,  but there are no granular methods that span the two

5: regular accounting software which isn't specialized for grants or science 
4: just the websites of the corporations but they aren't really competitive as they may even pay for being hosted elsewhere 

Phase 1: 
Basic social media space with an emphasis on posting scientific credentials, interests, and projects (LinkedIn and research gate style.). The draw and primary product is the ability to make custom collaboration smart contracts to formalize collaborations and expectations.

Phase 2: bounty board, methods repository, and method writing. 

The ability to insert into smart contracts methods either defined by the user in a txt/pdf or directly from a database of methods loaded from the tool itself. 

Allow the creation of "bounties" where someone will design a contract and post the method (with the level of transparency they desire) in a public space. The "rewards" for the bounty being authorship, money, etc. 

The ability to write methods into the repository and have them tagged as being designed by you (although most classic methods will still need citations) 

This will open the ability to start tagging users with metrics based on # of methods made, # of times people used your method, # of successful collaborations, etc. To give people ratings on the site
Phase 3: tool expansion

This is when things such as cloud flow would be powerful. 

This stage would be about implementing tools designed around in-house analysis. So instead of just uploading methods people can start uploading what are essentially orange work flows to show how they are going to analyze their data. This would also beef up profits as it would allow us to charge for computational time. 

The ability to interface with a TeamViewer like methodology so that collaborators can access an instrument/computer directly to run experiments. This would benefit greatly from connections in the automated science world. 

Lab notebooks, simple text processors with the capacity to store arbitrary data and link to smart contract contributions. (Ie. James Taylor did the mass spectrometry on a sample and uploaded it to the notebooks, while Jane Theop analyzed it and made the figure)

My experience was kind of unorthodox because I was co advised on a project I pitched by two adjunct professors from federal facilities
Willing to survey my network

High school student:
Knoweldge of how research works and access to labs where I could work on research in a practical manner in any way

Undergrad:
Access to research, the need to complete large swaths of projects without granular oversight, direct impact of my work on my future prospects (internships and general research time was nice but only "papers" and "presentations" counted, cost for work, cost for publishing, access to techniques and the like

Grad school:
Lack of expert mentors (I was interdisciplinary to a fault), lack of ability to post interesting work that did not have the support of my mentor for a full paper, complex and specific methods, lack of access to equipment that I need, lack of access to experts for what I was working on, jelousy in the research field (an old post-doc tried to scoop my thesis project, but he did it badly so it was fine because his data was bad), having to rewrite papers for each journal, adversarial reviewers (a japanese reviewer held up our paper for months and published a paper on a similar subject before ours went through), grant acceptance and review (need good data for the grants but it creates a circular requirement of basically doing the project before the grant was received), frankly changing format for each journal (This is a very powerful tool that would definitely pull in researchers to use the platform)

I most likely haven't thought as hard about these things as Emmett, but I'm going to try and argue for dual aspect monism here in the context of his article above: 
I tend to approximate the un-namable, eternal dao as something like david pierce's zero ontology: everything and nothing necessitate each other (unity in diversity). What I mean is that observable things/processes (names)(in physics and/or experience), have a beginning and end, which the eternal* dao (everything/nothing) lacks. As conscious beings, we notice that the only honest (frame invariant) picture of reality contains/is: everything and nothing, definite and indefinite, sequential and simultaneous, reducible and irreducible, computable and non-computable, etc.. Consciousness, having projected such a picture of reality from both ends of the epistemological spectrum (intro/extrospection, spirituality/science), is imo firmly enough to suggest it sits at least* as close to reality's substrate as physics does, similarly explicating the relativistic boundaries between implicate, interdependent absolutes. Reality is definitely relative, but the paradox is it can only be as relative as it is absolute; global frame dependence requires global frame invariance (uncertainty <-> certainty).

claude has this to say:

```Yes, it's possible to create this type of "meme vault" system using modern encryption techniques, though there are some challenges to consider.

You could implement this using a combination of symmetric encryption and content-based keys. Here's how it might work:

1. **Encrypted container file**: The core file would be encrypted using strong symmetric encryption (like AES-256).

2. **Content-based key derivation**: When someone adds a new image, you could use a cryptographic hash of that image (or some property of it) as part of the key derivation process to unlock the container.

3. **Chain of contributions**: Each new version of the file could include the previous content plus the new image, then be re-encrypted with a new key derived from the added content.

4. **Key sharing mechanism**: You'd need a system to share the necessary decryption information with the next person so they can add their contribution.

The main technical challenges would be:
- Ensuring the encryption remains secure while enabling the "contribution unlocks content" mechanism
- Handling file size as it grows with each contribution
- Preventing unauthorized sharing once someone has decrypted the file

As your friend noted, there's no perfect technical solution to prevent someone from simply extracting and sharing the content once they've unlocked it. That would rely on social norms and participant goodwill rather than technical constraints.

This is similar to how some artistic projects and crypto art experiments work - creating artificial scarcity or exclusivity through technological means while acknowledging that perfect control isn't possible.```

I think for bridging the gap between modules and making this a power tool for data analysis we could introduce the concept of datasets, which is basically a set of documents.

you start out producing a dataset by filtering the data from the whole db, then send it to an  module. that module allows you to select a subset of the data (e.g. 2d map) or to enrich it (e.g. exa API module) and outputs a new dataset.

then you can assemble pipelines using multiple tools for analysis + ingestion

Circled back to translation while reading *Babel* by R.F. Kuang. Also have been reading *Orality and Literacy* by Walter Ong. One of the key differences between the oral mode of thought and the literate mode of thought, I find, is that the former focuses immensely on fluency and flow, while the latter is concerned with accuracy and precision. While orality is time, literacy is space. We can think that orality is listening to the piece of music, while literacy is looking at the score. Literacy often breaks that same fluency and flow that is so essential to orality, while orality loses the ability to perform higher abstractions except on an iterative basis.

I strongly believe that my conversion to Christianity is largely due to my primarily literate English thought processes at last integrating the primarily oral mindset of Chinese, and (through psychedelics and meditation and other experiences) at last becoming able to see truth through a lens other than accuracy and precision using the most current folk ontology. It was fundamentally about leaving the illusion of literal primacy, and going back to that central hidden Word (beyond even the spoken word) at the center of it all.

Especially, the Trinity slowly crystallized itself in my inner world as a necessary truth about truth, the starting point for understanding all hermeneutics. For if there is any significance at all, and any sign of that significance, it must be through that spirit relating between sign and significance that we receive the significance through the sign. In the theological register, one must be moved by God the Holy Spirit to see that God the Son is the way to God the Father.

I could even imagine a Trinitarian religion apart from Christianity, to some extent. In fact, we can see the evolution of the Christian religion as exactly this. It is said that Christianity is a religion, but faith in Christ (and the Trinity) is not fundamentally religious. It is more a *personal* relationship to truth, a structure of interpretation and inspiration through which all reality can be investigated. Someone can be Christian without having faith in Christ (and the Trinity) and vice versa.

Christianity shall change, but I expect that the Trinity shall stay mostly static, as shall the Christology that makes Trinitarian theology mean anything in practice. The One True God we can know personally is necessarily the One True God who calls us to him through the united will of three persons.

Adam and Eve, cast from grace, could still speak and comprehend the language of angels. But when men pridefully built a path to heaven, God confounded their understanding. He divided and confused them, and scattered them about the face of the earth. The original primordial language was lost. It was something that could contain all meaning and was shared between all men. It is something like, language without any abstraction or displacement at all. Something like…touch? Scent?

This Adamic language is lost to us, but we can only think of language as of the tongue. Maybe the tongue is merely the best analogy? Who knows.

The real secret is that the way to recover what we lost at Babel is the Pentecost, which lets all human languages be united under one common Holy Spirit. This is where Christianity has been essential as the Rosetta Stone of all humanity. The need to translate God into every human tongue has of course caused the destruction of many oral cultures—Baudrillard in *Simulacra and Simulation* speaks of that paradox in ethnology where intimately studying uncontacted tribes makes them contacted, and *refusing* to study these tribes as in antiethnology is perhaps an even more artificial imposition, along the lines of artificially returning something to an “original” area after having originally artificially removed it from that location.

But in place of those oral cultures, Christianity as a new baseline literacy also enforces the creation of an oral culture under which all men are united in a timeless ontology, one which holds true across the ages and does not change according to temporal knowledge. Where science can give that accuracy and precision which literates love, religion and theology are the source of that fluency and flow which is essential to the passage of information. Because otherwise, we are indeed building a house upon the sand.

The ideal project of an empire is to unite everyone together and partake of that miracle of Pentecost, undoing the Babel that sundered a united mankind. However, there is true empire and false empire. Insofar as an empire is founded upon God, it is good and true. For reality is good and true. Insofar as an empire is not founded upon God, it has departed from truth and shall experience friction with the truth til it is resolved. Simulacra are destined to die, with only those true simulations being left behind.

APPENDIX:

God is not self-begetting, self-proceeding, or self-creating. The Trinity GOD is a self-referential (self-relating) system of three persons. At the very least, the doctrine forms such a self-referential construct.

[Singularity Hub — Each of the Brain’s Neurons Is Like Multiple Computers Running in Parallel](https://singularityhub.com/2025/04/21/each-of-the-brains-neurons-is-like-multiple-computers-running-in-parallel/)

> (H)ow do synapses know what adjustments best contribute to the neuron’s overall activity? Most only capture local information, yet somehow, they unite to tweak the cell’s output. “When people talk about synaptic plasticity, it’s typically regarded as uniform within the brain,” [said](https://www.eurekalert.org/news-releases/1080233) Wright. But learning initially occurs inside single synapses, each with its own personality.
> 
> Scientists have sought answer to this question—known as the credit assignment problem—by watching a handful of neurons in a dish or running simulations. But the neurons in these studies aren’t part of the brain-wide networks we use to learn, encode, and store memories, so they can’t capture how individual synapses contribute.
> 
> Over two weeks, the team captured activity from pyramidal cells—the ones with long branches on one end and bushes on the other. Rather than only observing each neuron’s activity as a whole, the team also watched individual synapses along each dendrite.
> 
> They didn’t behave the same way. Synapses on the longer branch closer to the top of the brain—known as the apical dendrite—rapidly synced with neighbors. Their connections strengthened and formed a tighter network.
> 
> “This indicates that learning-related plasticity is governed by local interactions between nearby synaptic inputs in apical dendrites,” wrote Groisman and Letzkus.
> 
> A neuron’s cell body—from which dendrites sprout—is also a computing machine. In another experiment, blocking the cell body’s action slashed signals from basal dendrites but not from apical dendrites. In other words, the neuron’s synapses functioned differently, depending on where they were. Some followed global activity in the cell; others cared more about local issues.

TL;DR — The two dendrites, apical and basal, receive different types of information from different areas of the brain and so either cares more about local issues or more about global issues. Each dendritic branch operates as an independent memory unit. Synapses, which transmit information between neurons and “dock” to each other to form connections during learning, unite to tweak a cell’s output; learning initially occurs inside single synapses, and then spreads to other cells as in a social network.

This is basically how humans learn too. There is top-down and bottom-up learning. Sometimes, there is a local solution—often found by one person—which first generalizes locally and then at last is enforced from on top.

Two paradigms in science keep going back and forth. In one, phenomena are described exactly according to how we predict the observations. (Einsteinian mechanics and so-called “heliocentrism” are this.) In the other, the relationships between the pointers for our concepts are operated on directly. It is hard to switch between these paradigms and hold them both in sight when the line between theology and science are eroded. This is why we need to keep a firm boundary between our baseline orality and constructs of truth, and the hypothetical operations we perform on top.

Right now, there is a faulty concept that what is reality is constantly changing, due to the scientific models for reality being entirely conflated with reality. This is philosophically fraught, socially erosive. Exactly why a part of all human knowledge must be thinking about God and doing our best to lay our groundwork on what reality even is, what is being investigated and tested, and what is the nature of our proofs and challenges and experiments and experiences. Of course, the *root* of our shift toward treating utilitarian science and knowledge as primary is this idea that work or production itself is virtue, or can even be said to be all of the human experience—justifying a sort of proletarian totalitarianism. Meanwhile, as Josef Pieper suggests in *Leisure, the Basis of Culture*, it is rest and observance—worship—that is virtuous. The type of person who gets nothing out of reflecting on the Trinity, on the basis that it has “no immediate applications”, is not going to “get” science and philosophy in the way that a believer in Christ would.

The Open Research Institute *must* work on the foundational philosophy of a society, and rather than upsetting long oral and cultural traditions must earnestly react to them without solely a mind for subterfuge. The purpose of working within these older paradigms is not to make them implode, but to redeem them and orient them toward truth-as-it-is—inevitability. Without a mind toward working on these foundations, and talking right at the furthest extreme of all “Why?” chains, the fruit of the Open Research Institute will be no more than the Library of Babel, a mess of symbols where everything is in theory said but nothing is understood.

In general, why I read more secondary literature on most topics over primary is because I think of philosophy and constructs as *tools* and not actually the basis of any truth. The writings on Hegel here are all not wrong in some sense, but also, I think what is most important is that the antithesis-synthesis loop is interesting as a hypothesis generator. Obviously such a generator can generate false garbage, but I still consider it fruitful to create more specimen to examine, to treat almost as artpieces to appraise and analyze. Is it okay to write down falsehoods over the course of brainstorming? I think so, and this is why I am more than happy to use whatever method from whatever philosophy that actually gets thought going and generates new statements to think over, even if it means we are only speaking of hypotheses and not actualities.

Maybe this is a key difference between how I think about writings and philosophy, and how many serious students of philosophy go about it. Mostly, I feel like students of philosophy take philosophy too seriously. (I as a student of Western classical art music take Western classical art music too seriously, which I try to account for.) What I see as the proposition of a new method is often seen solely as a metaphysical claim or something similar.

Textual manipulation itself is a kind of parable or allegorical writing. Whatever I write is fallible, but can be useful if it analogizes to infallible truths as revealed by God through the Holy Spirit.

I believe strongly enough that the foundations of the faith—the text of the Bible, the general authority of the one holy catholic “apostolic” church, the Trinity, Christology—are the best places for assurance that it is fine to mess around a little bit elsewhere, so long as we bring it back to those roots. I respect the Catholic view as stated by some (and mentioned or perhaps stated differently by Excom previously) that one ought to study philosophy before theology so as to open one’s mind to deep thought. However, *given* one’s assurance of the foundations, then the foundations of the faith ground one’s theology and this theology grounds one’s actual operational philosophy, and everything else is great fun and games.

When I play the piano or do creative writing, I sometimes play or write “badly” on purpose. Is it dishonest or misleading to do so? I do not think so, because it generates enough useful hypotheses (through my brain trying to reconcile what I am doing with my more systematic understanding) that can lead to something better. 

Moreover, in mathematics, it is normal to adopt unproven suppositions or even outside false (from outside the frame) statements under hypothesis in order to figure out how to move on the ground level. In the case of Christianity, the ground level is those foundations revealed by God, and we are *all* speaking under hypothesis under that. (People’s minds can be known and modeled to varying degrees of success as systems with regards to all outputs, and so can be interchangeably thought of, via analogy, as systems. By at least one possible abstraction, the purpose of a system is what it does.)

This happens to how I also think of philosophy as well as lay conversation between Christians. I think it is *fine* to use unorthodox frameworks under hypothesis. It is even *good* to do so if it strengthens one’s faith.

And so it should not be mistaken that I believe postmodernism actually has any special insight to the truth that the foundations of Christianity do not. Rather, I believe that postmodernism is a useful framework, and a *fun* framework. 

*If* a conversation were specifically about matters of doctrine and not just about sharing ideas, then maybe there should be a higher standard for the texts that are shared and produced. But I see this as unnecessarily flattening the terrain of all possible thinking.

There are some generating functions not for prime numbers, but for plausible candidates for prime numbers. If we treated these generating functions as simply outputting prime numbers, that would be a disaster, as one would start to use these candidates without question. But we do not have to treat these generating functions this way. We are allowed to simply use these things as toys. We are not forced to erroneously afford more authority to a system than we have to.

The lover, the worshipper, and the poet stand outside of the categories of rational usefulness and efficiency. Philosophy and love can be thought of in similar terms.

The philosopher and the poet are both concerned with the *mirandum*, the “wondrous”, the astonishing.

Worse than the extinguishing of the forms and methods of transcendence, the forceful sterilization of them, is their distortion into false forms of the original, pseudo-realizations of these basic experiences which only appear to pierce the canopy. 

There is a way to pray in which “this” world is not transcended, where one merely attempts to incorporate the divine as a functioning component of the machine of purposes. Religion is perverted into magic, and rather than becoming about self-dedication becomes the attempt to gain power over the divine, or to tap into this power and make it subservient to one’s own will. Prayer (and meditation, and the rest) become techniques for continuing to live life under the canopy.

Love can be narrowed so that the powers of self-giving become subservient to the goals of confined ego. There is no entry into a larger-deeper world, which only the truly loving person can enter.
(notes from reading *The Philosophical Act* by Josef Pieper)

Read *Leisure, the Basis of Culture*, and enjoy a nice little summary of how the modern concept of work and rest is degenerate, how it wants everyone to be proletariat, how it slowly erodes the ability for art and beauty to stand on its own. Then, wonder about why this so-called human doing is most prominent in what is the most materially abundant age in human history.

Having been convinced of the case that true leisure—coming from observance and presence and worship—is indeed the basis for culture, wonder about the duty you have to uphold good witness to the proper faithful way to be. Dennis Prager is wrong about a lot, but he is exactly right (from a conservative Jewish perspective) that everyone has the duty to be happy and truly fulfilled—not partaking in the trappings of happines and fulfillment, but actually being so.

Of course, much of what is apparently work and toil actually comes out of true flourishing. In those cases, we should simply see that the ontology of treating everything as work is a reflection of our modern times. We are in the age of “knowledge work” and the like, whereas the mark of a gentleman and an aristocrat was not “working”, but rather spending his days (and even efforts!) on callings such as the study of liberal arts, social service, prayer, or leadership.

Do I “work all day” when I type up all these notes? I think not. Rather, I am grateful (and privileged) to not have to work, to be able to spend my days thinking about God, about divine mysteries, about theology, about the church, about ORI, about systemic and textual experimentation I can try next.
Before any philosopher speaks, make them enunciate the theology that drives their philosophy.
